Data Processing Agreement Template
Data Processing Agreement
Template Version: 3.0 Last Updated: August 1, 2024 Prepared by: Acme Corp Legal Department
This Data Processing Agreement (“DPA”) forms part of the Enterprise Agreement (the “Agreement”) between Acme Corp (“Processor”) and the customer identified in the Agreement (“Controller”).
1. Definitions
Personal Data: Any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person, as defined under applicable data protection law.
Processing: Any operation performed on Personal Data, including collection, storage, retrieval, use, disclosure, erasure, or destruction.
Controller: The entity that determines the purposes and means of Processing Personal Data.
Processor: The entity that Processes Personal Data on behalf of the Controller.
Sub-Processor: A third party engaged by the Processor to Process Personal Data on behalf of the Controller.
Data Subject: The individual to whom Personal Data relates.
Supervisory Authority: An independent public authority responsible for monitoring the application of data protection law.
2. Roles and Responsibilities
2.1 Controller
The Controller determines the purposes and means of Processing the Personal Data covered by this DPA. The Controller is responsible for:
Ensuring that it has a lawful basis for the Processing it instructs the Processor to carry out.
Providing the Processor with documented instructions regarding the Processing.
Responding to Data Subject requests (with the Processor’s assistance as described in Section 6).
2.2 Processor
Acme Corp acts as Processor with respect to Personal Data that the Controller uploads to or processes through the Acme Corp platform. The Processor:
Processes Personal Data only on the documented instructions of the Controller, unless required to do so by applicable law.
Does not Process Personal Data for any purpose other than providing the services described in the Agreement.
Does not sell, rent, or otherwise make Personal Data available to third parties except as permitted in this DPA.
3. Sub-Processors
3.1 Authorization
The Controller provides general authorization for the Processor to engage Sub-Processors to assist in providing the services. The Processor maintains a list of current Sub-Processors, available upon request by emailing privacy@acmecorp.com.
3.2 Notification of Changes
The Processor will notify the Controller at least 30 days before adding or replacing a Sub-Processor. Notification is sent to the email address associated with the Controller’s account. The Controller may object to a new Sub-Processor within 15 days of notification by providing a written objection with reasonable grounds.
If the objection cannot be resolved, the Controller may terminate the affected service without penalty.
3.3 Sub-Processor Obligations
The Processor imposes data protection obligations on each Sub-Processor that are no less protective than those in this DPA. The Processor remains liable for the acts and omissions of its Sub-Processors.
3.4 Current Sub-Processors
As of the effective date of this DPA, the Processor uses the following categories of Sub-Processors:
The complete list of named Sub-Processors is provided in Annex C (not included in this template; populated per customer agreement).
4. Security Obligations
4.1 Technical and Organizational Measures
The Processor implements and maintains the following security measures:
Encryption of Personal Data at rest using AES-256.
Encryption of Personal Data in transit using TLS 1.3.
Access controls based on the principle of least privilege, with multi-factor authentication for all systems processing Personal Data.
Regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing (annual external, semi-annual internal).
Incident detection and response capabilities, including 24/7 monitoring.
Employee security awareness training (annual).
Physical security controls for data center facilities.
A detailed description of the Processor’s security measures is provided in Annex B (the Acme Corp IT Security Policy summary).
4.2 Confidentiality
The Processor ensures that personnel authorized to Process Personal Data are subject to contractual confidentiality obligations. Access to Personal Data is limited to personnel who require it to perform their duties.
5. Breach Notification
5.1 Notification to Controller
In the event of a Personal Data breach, the Processor will notify the Controller without undue delay, and in any event within 48 hours of becoming aware of the breach. The notification will include:
A description of the nature of the breach, including the categories and approximate number of Data Subjects and records affected.
The name and contact details of the Processor’s Data Protection Officer.
A description of the likely consequences of the breach.
A description of the measures taken or proposed to address the breach and mitigate its effects.
5.2 Cooperation
The Processor will cooperate with the Controller in investigating the breach, notifying Supervisory Authorities and Data Subjects as required by applicable law, and implementing remedial measures.
5.3 Record Keeping
The Processor maintains a record of all Personal Data breaches, including the facts of the breach, its effects, and the remedial actions taken. This record is available to the Controller and Supervisory Authorities upon request.
6. Data Subject Rights
The Processor assists the Controller in fulfilling its obligations to respond to Data Subject requests (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection). Assistance includes:
Providing the Controller with the ability to access, export, correct, and delete Data Subject records through the platform’s administrative tools.
Redirecting Data Subject requests received directly by the Processor to the Controller within 3 business days.
Providing technical support for complex requests (e.g., large-scale data exports) within 10 business days.
7. Audit Rights
7.1 Audit by Controller
The Controller has the right to audit the Processor’s compliance with this DPA. Audits may be conducted:
By the Controller or a qualified third-party auditor appointed by the Controller.
No more than once per 12-month period, unless a breach or material non-compliance has occurred.
With at least 30 days’ written notice.
During normal business hours and without unreasonably disrupting the Processor’s operations.
7.2 Audit Reports
As an alternative to an on-site audit, the Processor will make available:
The most recent SOC 2 Type II report.
The most recent ISO 27001 certificate and statement of applicability.
A completed security questionnaire (SIG Lite or equivalent) upon request.
If these documents are sufficient to address the Controller’s audit objectives, the Controller agrees to accept them in lieu of an on-site audit.
8. Data Transfers
8.1 Transfer Mechanisms
If Personal Data is transferred outside the European Economic Area (EEA), the Processor ensures that appropriate safeguards are in place, including:
Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) approved by the European Commission (Module 2: Controller to Processor).
Any supplementary measures necessary to ensure an essentially equivalent level of protection.
8.2 Data Residency
The Controller may specify data residency requirements in the Order Form. Available regions: US-East (default) and EU-West. The Processor will not transfer Personal Data outside the specified region except as necessary for Sub-Processor processing described in Section 3.
9. Term and Termination
This DPA remains in effect for the duration of the Agreement. Upon termination of the Agreement:
The Processor deletes all Personal Data within 90 days per the Customer Deletion Workflow.
The Controller may request a data export before deletion (see the Customer Deletion Workflow, Section 3).
The Processor provides a Deletion Certificate upon completion.
Sections that by their nature should survive termination (confidentiality, audit rights for the retention period, breach notification for incidents discovered post-termination) survive for 3 years after termination.
10. Governing Law
This DPA is governed by the laws specified in the Agreement. For Data Subjects in the EEA, the provisions of the GDPR apply regardless of the governing law of the Agreement.
DPA Template v3.0 — Last updated August 1, 2024. For execution, contact legal@acmecorp.com.

# Data Protection Officer — Role Description

**Effective Date:** December 1, 2024
**Current DPO:** Mariana Costa
**Applies to:** Acme Corp organization-wide

---

## 1. Purpose

The Data Protection Officer (DPO) is responsible for overseeing Acme Corp's data protection strategy and ensuring compliance with applicable data protection laws, including GDPR, HIPAA, and state-level privacy regulations. The role was established to meet the requirements of GDPR Article 37, which mandates the designation of a DPO for organizations that carry out large-scale processing of personal data.

## 2. Scope

The DPO's mandate covers:

- All personal data processed by Acme Corp, whether as a data controller (employee data, prospect data) or as a data processor (customer data processed on behalf of enterprise customers under a Data Processing Agreement).
- All Acme Corp systems that store or process personal data, across all regions (US-East, EU-West).
- All departments that handle personal data, including Engineering, Customer Success, Sales, Marketing, and Human Resources.

## 3. Responsibilities

### 3.1 Advisory

- Advise the company on its obligations under data protection law.
- Provide guidance on data protection impact assessments (DPIAs) for new products, features, or processing activities that involve personal data.
- Review and approve data retention schedules, deletion procedures, and anonymization processes. The DPO is the designated owner of the Data Retention Policy.
- Advise on data transfer mechanisms for international data flows (Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules).

### 3.2 Monitoring and Audit

- Monitor compliance with GDPR, HIPAA, and internal data protection policies.
- Conduct or commission annual data protection audits.
- Review the data processing register and ensure it is complete and current.
- Verify deletions executed under the Data Retention Policy and the Customer Deletion Workflow.

### 3.3 Data Subject Rights

- Oversee the process for responding to data subject access requests (DSARs), erasure requests, and portability requests.
- Ensure that responses are issued within the legally required timeframe (30 days under GDPR, with a possible 60-day extension for complex requests).

### 3.4 Breach Response

- Serve as the internal point of contact for data breach assessment and notification.
- Determine whether a breach meets the threshold for notification to supervisory authorities (GDPR: 72 hours) and affected individuals.
- Coordinate with the Chief Compliance Officer and Legal on regulatory notifications.

### 3.5 Training

- Develop and maintain the data privacy training curriculum for all employees.
- Ensure that employees who handle personal data complete annual data privacy training per the Mandatory Training Catalog.
- Deliver specialized training for teams with elevated data access (Engineering, Customer Success, Sales).

## 4. Reporting Line

The DPO reports directly to the Board of Directors through the Audit Committee. This reporting line ensures independence from operational management, as required by GDPR Article 38.

The DPO also has a working relationship with:

- The Chief Compliance Officer (Jordan Patel), for coordination on regulatory matters.
- The VP of Engineering (Sarah Chen), for coordination on technical data protection measures.
- The General Counsel, for coordination on legal matters and data subject litigation.

The DPO cannot be dismissed or penalized for performing their duties. Any proposed changes to the DPO role must be reviewed by the Audit Committee.

## 5. Qualifications and Certifications

The DPO must hold:

- A law degree, privacy certification, or equivalent professional qualification in data protection.
- At least one of the following certifications: CIPP/E (Certified Information Privacy Professional — Europe), CIPM (Certified Information Privacy Manager), or CDPSE (Certified Data Privacy Solutions Engineer).

The current DPO, Mariana Costa, holds a Master of Laws (LLM) in Information Technology and Privacy Law from Tilburg University and is certified as CIPP/E and CIPM.

## 6. Ongoing Professional Development

The DPO is required to:

- Complete at least 20 hours of continuing privacy education per year.
- Attend at least one data protection conference or regulatory workshop annually.
- Maintain active certification status with the IAPP (International Association of Privacy Professionals).

Training costs are funded by the Legal department budget and are not subject to the standard employee tuition reimbursement cap.

## 7. Resources

The DPO has authority to:

- Access all data processing activities and all personal data held by Acme Corp, without restriction.
- Engage external legal counsel or specialist consultants for complex data protection matters, with budget approval from the General Counsel.
- Escalate concerns directly to the Board of Directors if internal remediation is insufficient.

The DPO is supported by one Privacy Analyst who assists with DSAR processing, audit coordination, and register maintenance.

## 8. Contact

The DPO can be reached at dpo@acmecorp.com. For general privacy questions, employees should contact privacy@acmecorp.com.

---

*Version 2.0 — Last revised December 1, 2024*
title: Data Protection Officer — Role Description

text:
# Data Retention Policy

**Document ID:** COMP-001
**Effective Date:** January 1, 2024
**Approved by:** Chief Compliance Officer, General Counsel
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp employees, contractors, and systems that process, store, or transmit company or customer data

---

## General Requirements

All customer data must be retained for a minimum of 7 years from the date of the last transaction. This applies to all data categories listed in section 4.2, with the exception of anonymized datasets processed after the effective date of this policy (January 1, 2024).

The 7-year retention requirement covers:

- **Financial records:** Transaction logs, invoices, payment records, revenue recognition data, and audit evidence. Retention is driven by SOX compliance, IRS requirements, and contractual obligations.
- **Customer account data:** Account profiles, subscription history, usage records, and support ticket archives.
- **Contractual records:** Executed agreements, amendments, order forms, and statements of work.
- **Compliance records:** Audit logs, compliance reports, training completion records, and policy acknowledgment receipts.

### Anonymized Data Exception

Anonymized datasets — defined as datasets from which all personally identifiable information (PII) has been irreversibly removed such that no individual can be re-identified — are exempt from the 7-year retention requirement. Anonymized datasets may be retained indefinitely for analytics, research, and product improvement purposes, or deleted at the discretion of the data owner.

The Data Protection Officer must certify that the anonymization process meets the irreversibility standard before a dataset is reclassified as anonymized. Pseudonymized data (where re-identification is possible with a separate key) is NOT considered anonymized and remains subject to the 7-year rule.

### Retention Triggers

The retention clock starts on the date of the last transaction or the last meaningful activity on the record, whichever is later. For customer accounts, "last transaction" means the last subscription payment, the last login, or the contract end date — whichever occurs latest.

If a record is referenced in an active legal hold, audit, or investigation, the retention period is extended until the hold is released, regardless of the standard retention schedule.

---

## EU-Specific Requirements

For customers subject to GDPR, data retention is further constrained by the principle of storage limitation. Personal data may only be retained for as long as necessary for the purpose for which it was collected. The 7-year general requirement applies only to transaction records; personal identification data must be deleted or anonymized within 3 years unless a specific legal basis for longer retention exists.

### What Qualifies as Personal Identification Data

Under this policy, personal identification data includes:

- Name, email address, phone number, mailing address.
- Government-issued identifiers (tax ID, national insurance number) — where collected.
- IP addresses and device identifiers associated with a specific individual.
- Any data that, alone or in combination, can identify a natural person.

### 3-Year Retention Limit

The 3-year limit applies from the date the personal data was last processed for its original purpose. For example:

- A customer's contact information collected during onboarding must be deleted or anonymized within 3 years of the customer's last contract end date.
- Marketing consent records must be retained for the duration of the consent plus 3 years for compliance evidence.

EU customer data stored in the EU-West region is subject to automated retention monitoring. The compliance system flags records approaching the 3-year threshold 90 days in advance, giving the data owner time to confirm deletion or document a lawful basis for continued retention.

### Legal Basis for Extended Retention

Retaining EU personal data beyond 3 years requires a documented legal basis, which must be one of:

- Performance of a contract (active customer relationship).
- Legal obligation (regulatory retention requirement).
- Legitimate interest (documented and balanced against the data subject's rights).

The legal basis must be recorded in the data processing register and reviewed annually by the Data Protection Officer.

---

## Data Deletion Procedures

Upon expiration of the retention period, data must be permanently deleted within 90 calendar days. Deletion must be verified by the Data Protection Officer and logged in the compliance audit trail. Backup copies must be purged within 180 days of the primary deletion.

### Deletion Process

The deletion process consists of four steps:

1. **Identification.** The compliance monitoring system generates a deletion queue of records that have reached the end of their retention period.
2. **Review.** The data owner reviews the queue and confirms that no active legal hold, audit, or contractual obligation requires continued retention.
3. **Execution.** IT executes the deletion across all primary storage systems (production databases, file storage, search indexes). Deletion uses cryptographic erasure where supported (destroying the encryption key for the data segment) or NIST 800-88 compliant media sanitization.
4. **Verification.** The Data Protection Officer verifies that the data has been removed from primary systems and certifies the deletion in the compliance audit trail.

### Backup Purge

Backup copies containing deleted data must be purged within 180 days of the primary deletion. Because backups are typically stored as immutable snapshots, the standard approach is to allow the backup retention cycle to expire naturally (backups are retained for 90 days per the Backup and Recovery Policy). If a specific backup contains Restricted data that has been deleted from primary systems, the backup may be flagged for early expiration.

### Deletion Logging

Every deletion action is logged with:

- Record identifier(s) deleted.
- Date and time of deletion.
- Deletion method (cryptographic erasure, overwrite, physical destruction).
- Name of the person who executed the deletion.
- Name of the Data Protection Officer who verified the deletion.

Deletion logs are retained for 7 years as compliance evidence.

---

## Responsibilities

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| Data Owner (department head) | Classify data, confirm retention periods, review deletion queues |
| Data Protection Officer | Certify anonymization, verify deletions, review EU retention extensions, maintain the data processing register |
| IT Operations | Execute deletions, manage backup purge cycles, maintain the compliance monitoring system |
| Legal | Advise on legal holds, review retention extensions, maintain the legal hold register |
| Chief Compliance Officer | Approve policy changes, present compliance metrics to the board, oversee audit responses |

---

## Related Documents

- Customer Deletion Workflow — process for deleting customer data upon account termination
- Backup and Recovery Policy — backup retention schedules and recovery procedures
- Data Classification Policy — data classification tiers and handling requirements
- Audit Log Retention — audit log-specific retention (1 year hot, 6 years cold)
- EU Office Data Retention Schedule — EU-specific retention schedule with additional local requirements
- US Office Data Retention Schedule — US-specific overrides for state-law compliance

---

## Policy Governance

This policy is reviewed annually by Legal, the Data Protection Officer, and the Chief Compliance Officer (Jordan Patel). The next scheduled review is January 2025. Questions should be directed to compliance@acmecorp.com.

---

*Document version 3.0 — Last revised January 1, 2024*
title: Data Retention Policy

text:
# Database Operations Runbook

**Effective Date:** December 1, 2024
**Owner:** Database Engineering team
**Applies to:** All PostgreSQL production clusters managed by Acme Corp

---

## 1. Database Architecture

Acme Corp runs PostgreSQL 16 as its primary relational database. The production environment consists of:

| Component | Configuration | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Primary instance | db.r6g.4xlarge, 1 TB SSD | Handles all write operations |
| Read replica 1 | db.r6g.2xlarge, 1 TB SSD | Serves read traffic from the application layer |
| Read replica 2 | db.r6g.2xlarge, 1 TB SSD | Serves read traffic from analytics and reporting workloads |
| pgvector replica | db.r6g.4xlarge, 1 TB SSD | Dedicated to semantic search (HNSW vector index) |

All instances run in the US-East region with synchronous replication to the primary read replica and asynchronous replication to the others.

---

## 2. Backups

### 2.1 Backup Schedule

| Backup Type | Frequency | Retention | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous WAL archiving | Real-time | 30 days of point-in-time recovery | S3 (same region) |
| Full snapshot | Daily at 2:00 AM UTC | 90 days | S3 (same region + cross-region replica) |

### 2.2 Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR)

PITR allows restoring the database to any point within the 30-day WAL retention window. To perform a PITR:

1. Create a new instance from the most recent daily snapshot that precedes the target recovery point.
2. Apply WAL records up to the desired timestamp.
3. Verify data integrity (row counts, application-level checksums).
4. Promote the new instance to primary if replacing the current production instance, or use it for data extraction.

Recovery Point Objective (RPO): 1 hour. Recovery Time Objective (RTO): 4 hours.

### 2.3 Backup Verification

The database engineering team tests backup restoration quarterly per the Backup and Recovery Policy. Each test restores a full snapshot plus WAL replay to a temporary instance and runs the application smoke test suite against it.

---

## 3. Failover

### 3.1 Automated Failover

If the primary instance becomes unresponsive:

1. The monitoring system (Datadog) detects the failure within 30 seconds.
2. The connection proxy (PgBouncer) stops sending new connections to the primary.
3. Read replica 1 (synchronous) is promoted to primary automatically. Promotion takes 15–60 seconds.
4. The application reconnects through the proxy with no configuration change required.
5. An alert is sent to the DBA on-call and the #database-ops Slack channel.

### 3.2 Manual Failover

For planned maintenance (major version upgrades, hardware changes):

1. Announce the maintenance window at least 72 hours in advance per the Enterprise SLA.
2. Stop write traffic by setting the primary to read-only mode.
3. Wait for all replicas to catch up (replication lag = 0).
4. Promote the target replica to primary.
5. Update the proxy configuration to point to the new primary.
6. Verify application health. Resume write traffic.

Planned failover typically causes < 30 seconds of write unavailability. Reads continue without interruption.

---

## 4. Query Tuning

### 4.1 Identifying Slow Queries

The `pg_stat_statements` extension tracks query execution statistics. Use the following query to find the slowest queries by total time:

```sql
SELECT query,
       calls,
       mean_exec_time,
       total_exec_time,
       rows
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 20;
```

Queries with `mean_exec_time` above 100ms or `total_exec_time` in the top 20 are candidates for optimization.

The Datadog PostgreSQL integration provides a real-time dashboard of query performance, including p50, p95, and p99 latency by query fingerprint.

### 4.2 Reading Execution Plans

Use `EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)` to examine how PostgreSQL executes a query:

```sql
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT * FROM records WHERE account_id = 'acct_a1b2c3' AND status = 'active';
```

Key things to look for:

- **Seq Scan on a large table:** Indicates a missing index. The database is reading every row instead of using an index to jump directly to matching rows.
- **Nested Loop with high row estimates:** May indicate a missing join index or a statistics issue.
- **Sort with high memory usage:** Consider adding an index on the sort column, or increasing `work_mem` for the session.
- **Bitmap Heap Scan with many recheck conditions:** The index is filtering some rows but PostgreSQL still needs to verify each one against the heap — common with low-selectivity conditions.

### 4.3 Ways to Make Database Lookups Faster

When a query is slow, work through the following checklist in order:

1. **Add a missing index.** The most common fix. If the query filters on a column that is not indexed, creating a B-tree index on that column typically reduces lookup time from seconds to milliseconds. For composite filters (e.g., `WHERE account_id = ? AND status = ?`), create a multi-column index with the most selective column first.

2. **Use a covering index.** If the query selects only a few columns, an `INCLUDE` index that covers all selected columns allows PostgreSQL to answer the query entirely from the index without reading the table heap. This eliminates random I/O and can speed up lookups by 2–5x for read-heavy workloads.

3. **Partition large tables.** Tables with more than 100 million rows benefit from range partitioning (typically by date). Partitioning allows PostgreSQL to skip entire partitions during query planning, making lookups faster by reducing the search space.

4. **Tune connection pooling.** If lookup latency is high but the execution plan is fast, the bottleneck may be connection acquisition. PgBouncer is configured for transaction-level pooling with a maximum of 200 connections to the primary. If the pool is saturated, queries queue. Monitor `pgbouncer_pool_waiting_clients` in Datadog.

5. **Increase shared_buffers and effective_cache_size.** If the working set does not fit in memory, PostgreSQL reads from disk. Our production configuration: `shared_buffers = 16GB` (25% of instance RAM), `effective_cache_size = 48GB` (75% of instance RAM). These values are reviewed during quarterly capacity planning.

6. **Use read replicas for analytics queries.** Heavy analytical queries (aggregations, full-table scans for reporting) should run against read replica 2, not the primary. This prevents analytics workloads from competing with transactional queries for CPU and I/O. Application code routes queries based on the `read_only=true` connection parameter.

7. **Consider materialized views.** For complex queries that aggregate data across multiple tables and are run frequently with the same parameters, a materialized view pre-computes the result. Refresh the view on a schedule (e.g., every 6 hours) rather than computing it on every query. Trade-off: data freshness vs. query speed.

### 4.4 Index Maintenance

- **Reindex bloated indexes** quarterly or when index bloat exceeds 30% (measured by `pgstattuple`). Use `REINDEX CONCURRENTLY` to avoid blocking writes.
- **Remove unused indexes.** Indexes that are never scanned (check `pg_stat_user_indexes.idx_scan = 0` over a 30-day period) waste storage and slow down writes. Drop them after confirming with the application team.
- **Monitor index usage** in the Datadog PostgreSQL dashboard. Alert when a new query introduces a sequential scan on a table with more than 1 million rows.

---

## 5. Indexing Strategies

### 5.1 B-Tree Indexes

The default index type. Optimal for equality and range queries. Use for:

- Primary keys and foreign keys (created automatically).
- Columns used in WHERE clauses with `=`, `<`, `>`, `BETWEEN`, `IN`.
- Columns used in ORDER BY and GROUP BY.

### 5.2 GIN Indexes

Generalized Inverted Indexes. Optimal for full-text search, JSONB containment queries, and array operations. Used in Acme Corp for:

- Full-text search on record content (`to_tsvector` / `to_tsquery`).
- JSONB metadata field queries (`metadata @> '{"department": "Engineering"}'`).

### 5.3 HNSW Indexes (pgvector)

Hierarchical Navigable Small World indexes for approximate nearest neighbor search on vector columns. Used by the semantic search feature. See the ML Platform Architecture document for HNSW parameter tuning (`ef_construction`, `M`, `ef_search`) and performance benchmarks.

### 5.4 BRIN Indexes

Block Range Indexes. Effective for large tables where the indexed column is naturally correlated with the physical order of the rows (e.g., timestamps on append-only tables). BRIN indexes are much smaller than B-tree indexes and are used on the audit log table (`created_at` column).

---

## 6. Common Operational Tasks

| Task | Command / Procedure | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Check replication lag | `SELECT now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS lag;` on each replica | Continuous (Datadog monitor) |
| Kill a long-running query | `SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid);` after identifying the PID from `pg_stat_activity` | As needed |
| Vacuum a table | `VACUUM (ANALYZE) table_name;` — updates statistics and reclaims dead tuples | Autovacuum handles most cases; manual vacuum for bulk-delete operations |
| Check table bloat | `SELECT * FROM pgstattuple('table_name');` | Monthly |
| Rotate database credentials | Rotate via HashiCorp Vault; application picks up new credentials automatically via Vault agent | Every 180 days per IT Security Policy |

---

## 7. Escalation

| Situation | Escalate To |
|---|---|
| Replication lag > 30 seconds | DBA on-call |
| Primary instance unresponsive | DBA on-call + Platform on-call (automated alert) |
| Data corruption suspected | DBA on-call + VP of Engineering |
| Query performance degradation affecting SLO | DBA on-call + service owner |

See the Incident Response Runbook for the full escalation process.

---

*Version 3.0 — Last revised December 1, 2024. Questions: database-eng@acmecorp.com.*
title: Database Operations Runbook

text:
# Deployment Runbook

**Effective Date:** November 15, 2024
**Owner:** Engineering Platform team
**Applies to:** All production deployments for Acme Corp services

---

## 1. Deployment Strategy

Acme Corp uses a blue-green deployment model with canary rollout for all production services.

- **Blue environment:** The current live production environment serving all customer traffic.
- **Green environment:** The standby environment where the new version is deployed and verified before receiving traffic.

A deployment proceeds through three phases: canary, progressive rollout, and full cutover.

## 2. Pre-Deployment Checklist

Before initiating a deployment:

- [ ] All CI checks pass on the release branch (lint, unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end smoke suite).
- [ ] Database migrations (if any) have been applied to the staging environment and verified.
- [ ] The change has been approved through the code review process per the Code Review Guidelines.
- [ ] The deployment is logged in the #deployments Slack channel with: service name, version, deployer, and link to the changelog.
- [ ] Confirm that no active SEV1 or SEV2 incident is in progress. If one is, defer the deployment unless the deployment itself is the incident fix.
- [ ] Confirm the deployment window: standard deployments are allowed Monday–Thursday, 9 AM – 3 PM PT. Friday deployments require Platform team approval.

## 3. Canary Rollout

1. Deploy the new version to the green environment.
2. Run the post-deployment verification suite against the green environment (see Section 6).
3. Route **5% of production traffic** to the green environment via the load balancer.
4. Monitor for 15 minutes. Check:
   - Error rate: must not exceed baseline by more than 0.1%.
   - Latency (p99): must not exceed baseline by more than 50ms.
   - No new error types in Sentry.
   - No anomalous patterns in Datadog dashboards.
5. If metrics are healthy, proceed to progressive rollout.
6. If metrics degrade, trigger an immediate rollback (Section 5).

## 4. Progressive Rollout

After a successful canary phase:

| Step | Traffic to Green | Duration | Gate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5% | 15 minutes | Automated metric check |
| 2 | 25% | 15 minutes | Automated metric check |
| 3 | 50% | 15 minutes | Automated metric check |
| 4 | 100% | — | Manual confirmation by deployer |

At each step, the deployment pipeline automatically verifies that error rate and latency remain within tolerance. If any check fails, rollout is paused and the deployer is notified.

The final step (100% cutover) requires the deployer to manually confirm in the deployment dashboard. This prevents unattended full rollouts.

## 5. Rollback Procedure

If a deployment causes degradation at any point:

1. **Immediate rollback:** Click **Rollback** in the deployment dashboard, or run `acme-deploy rollback --service <name>`. This shifts 100% of traffic back to the blue environment within 30 seconds.
2. **Verify rollback:** Confirm that metrics return to baseline within 5 minutes.
3. **Notify:** Post in #deployments and #incidents with the rollback reason.
4. **Investigate:** The deployer opens a post-deployment investigation ticket. Root cause must be identified before re-attempting the deployment.

Rollback is always available for 24 hours after a full cutover. After 24 hours, the blue environment is updated to match green (becoming the new baseline), and rollback to the previous version requires a new deployment.

### Database Migrations and Rollback

Database migrations must be backward-compatible. The previous application version must be able to run against the new database schema. This ensures that a rollback does not require a database rollback (which is risky and slow).

If a migration is not backward-compatible, it must be deployed in two phases:
1. Deploy the migration separately (additive change only — new columns, new tables, no drops).
2. Deploy the application code that uses the new schema.
3. In a subsequent deployment, remove the old columns/tables after confirming no code references them.

## 6. Post-Deployment Verification

After full cutover (100% traffic on green), run the verification suite:

- **Smoke tests:** Automated suite that exercises critical user paths (login, create record, search, export). Must pass within 5 minutes.
- **Synthetic monitoring:** Datadog synthetic checks confirm that key API endpoints respond correctly from multiple geographic locations.
- **Manual spot checks:** The deployer manually verifies 2–3 user-facing flows in production. Document the checks in the deployment log.

If verification fails after full cutover, trigger an immediate rollback.

## 7. Kill Switches

Kill switches allow specific features to be disabled in production without a deployment:

- Kill switches are implemented as feature flags in the configuration service.
- Every new user-facing feature must ship behind a kill switch.
- Kill switches are toggled in the deployment dashboard or via CLI: `acme-config set --flag <name> --value false`.
- The effect is immediate (propagated within 60 seconds via the configuration service).

Kill switches remain in the codebase for 30 days after the feature reaches 100% rollout. After 30 days, the Platform team removes the switch in a cleanup PR.

## 8. Deployment Schedule

| Window | Allowed | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Monday–Thursday, 9 AM – 3 PM PT | Yes (standard) | No approval required |
| Thursday, 3 PM – Friday EOD | Discouraged | Requires Platform team approval |
| Weekends | Emergency only | Requires VP of Engineering approval |
| During active SEV1/SEV2 | No (unless the deploy is the fix) | Incident commander must approve |

## 9. Contacts

- Deployment issues: #deployments Slack channel
- Platform team: platform-eng@acmecorp.com
- Incident escalation: see the Incident Response Runbook

---

*Version 3.0 — Last revised November 15, 2024*
title: Deployment Runbook

text:
# Development Workflow

**Effective Date:** November 1, 2024
**Owner:** Engineering Platform team
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp engineering teams

---

## 1. Branching Strategy

Acme Corp uses a trunk-based development workflow with short-lived feature branches:

- **`main`** is the trunk. It is always in a deployable state. Direct commits to `main` are blocked; all changes go through pull requests.
- **Feature branches** are created from `main` for each unit of work (feature, bug fix, refactor). Branch naming convention: `<type>/<ticket-id>-<short-description>` (e.g., `feat/ENG-1234-add-hybrid-search`, `fix/ENG-5678-null-pointer-export`).
- Feature branches should be short-lived: merge within 2–3 days. Branches open longer than 5 days are flagged in the weekly engineering standup.
- **Long-lived branches** (release branches, hotfix branches) are used only for coordinated releases and emergency fixes. They follow the same review and CI requirements as feature branches.

## 2. Pull Request Workflow

1. Engineer creates a feature branch and implements the change.
2. Engineer opens a pull request against `main`. The PR description must include: what the change does, why it is needed, how to test it, and links to the relevant ticket and design document (if applicable).
3. CI runs automatically on the PR: lint, unit tests, integration tests, and security scanning.
4. A code owner reviews the PR per the Code Review Guidelines.
5. If the change touches security-sensitive areas, additional review is required (see Section 4).
6. Once approved, the PR enters the merge queue. The merge queue rebases onto `main`, runs CI again, and merges if all checks pass.

## 3. CI/CD Pipeline

### 3.1 Continuous Integration

Every pull request triggers the following CI checks:

| Stage | Tool | Purpose | Blocking? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formatting | ruff format / gofmt / prettier | Code style consistency | Yes |
| Linting | ruff / golangci-lint / eslint | Static analysis, code quality | Yes |
| Unit tests | pytest / go test / jest | Functional correctness | Yes |
| Integration tests | pytest (with test database) | End-to-end path verification | Yes |
| Security scan | Snyk / Dependabot | Dependency vulnerabilities | Yes (critical/high) |
| Coverage check | coverage.py / go tool cover | Minimum 80% line coverage | Yes |

All stages must pass before a PR can be approved and merged. There are no manual overrides for CI failures except during a hotfix (see the Deployment Runbook).

### 3.2 Continuous Deployment

After a PR merges to `main`:

1. The deployment pipeline builds a container image tagged with the commit SHA.
2. The image is pushed to the container registry.
3. The image is deployed to the staging environment automatically.
4. Automated smoke tests run against staging.
5. If smoke tests pass, the deployment proceeds to production via the canary rollout process described in the Deployment Runbook.

Average time from merge to production: 25 minutes (including canary observation).

## 4. Security Review Checkpoint

Certain categories of changes require a mandatory security review by the Information Security (InfoSec) team before the PR can be merged. This checkpoint ensures that security-sensitive changes are evaluated by a specialist in addition to the standard code review.

### 4.1 Changes Requiring Security Review

A security review is required for any PR that modifies:

- **Authentication or authorization logic:** Changes to the Auth Service, API key validation, OAuth flows, permission checks, or session management.
- **Encryption or key management:** Changes to encryption algorithms, key rotation logic, TLS configuration, or secrets management integration (HashiCorp Vault).
- **Data access patterns:** New database queries that access Confidential or Restricted data, new API endpoints that expose PII or PHI, or changes to data export functionality.
- **Infrastructure security:** Changes to firewall rules, network segmentation, VPN configuration, or production access controls.
- **Dependency additions:** Adding a new third-party library that processes sensitive data or runs with elevated privileges.

The PR author is responsible for identifying whether a security review is needed. CI includes a label check: PRs that modify files in designated security-sensitive paths are automatically labeled `security-review-required` and cannot be merged without an approval from a member of the InfoSec team.

### 4.2 Security Review Process

1. The PR is labeled `security-review-required` (automatically or manually).
2. The InfoSec team is notified via the #security-reviews Slack channel.
3. An InfoSec engineer reviews the change within 2 business days. The review focuses on:
   - Compliance with the IT Security Policy (password handling, encryption standards, access control requirements).
   - Adherence to OWASP Top 10 guidelines (injection, broken auth, XSS, etc.).
   - Proper use of security libraries and APIs.
   - Potential for data leakage or privilege escalation.
4. The InfoSec engineer approves, requests changes, or escalates to the InfoSec team lead for complex cases.
5. The PR cannot merge until the security review is resolved.

### 4.3 Interaction with the IT Security Policy

The security review checkpoint is a control required by the IT Security Policy (Section 8 — Vulnerability Management and Patching, and Section 4 — Access Controls). It ensures that changes to security-sensitive code are reviewed by personnel with security expertise, in addition to the functional code review by the service's code owner.

The InfoSec team maintains a list of security-sensitive file paths per repository. This list is updated quarterly and published in the engineering wiki. When a new service is created, the service owner and the InfoSec team jointly define which paths require security review.

## 5. Environments

| Environment | Purpose | Deployment | Data |
|---|---|---|---|
| Local | Developer workstation | Manual (docker-compose) | Synthetic seed data |
| CI | Automated testing | On every PR | Ephemeral test database |
| Staging | Pre-production validation | Automatic on merge to `main` | Anonymized copy of production data (refreshed weekly) |
| Production | Customer-facing | Canary rollout from staging | Real customer data |

Engineers do not have direct write access to the production environment. All production changes go through the CI/CD pipeline and the canary rollout process. Read access to production logs and metrics is available through Datadog and Grafana. Direct database access requires a documented justification and goes through the database proxy with full session recording per the Access Control Policy.

## 6. Hotfix Process

For emergency fixes during an active SEV1 or SEV2 incident:

1. Create a branch from `main`.
2. Implement the fix with a minimal, focused change.
3. Obtain one approval from any available senior engineer (L4+) — the standard code review process is completed retroactively within 24 hours.
4. Merge and deploy directly to production, bypassing the canary observation period if the incident commander approves.
5. Document the hotfix in the incident channel and link it to the postmortem.

## 7. Policy Governance

This workflow is maintained by the Engineering Platform team. Changes are discussed in the biweekly engineering standards review meeting. Questions: eng-standards@acmecorp.com.

---

*Version 2.0 — Last revised November 1, 2024*
title: Development Workflow

Acme Corp Employee Handbook
Acme Corp Employee Handbook
Effective Date: January 15, 2025 Supersedes: All prior editions Applies to: All Acme Corp
employees (full-time, part-time, and temporary)
Chapter 1 — Employment Terms
1.1 At-Will Employment
Employment at Acme Corp is at-will unless otherwise specified in a written employment agree-
ment. Either the employee or the company may terminate the employment relationship at any
time, with or without cause, and with or without notice.
1.2 Probationary Period
All new hires enter a 90-day probationary period beginning on their first day of employment.
During this period:
• The employee’s manager conducts check-ins at the 30-day and 60-day marks.
• A formal probationary review meeting occurs at or before day 90.
• Successful completion of the probationary period is required before the employee is eligi-
ble for internal transfers, tuition reimbursement, or the employee referral bonus program.
• Termination during the probationary period may occur without the standard performance
improvement process.
Managers must submit a probationary review form to Human Resources no later than the em-
ployee’s 85th day.
1.3 Employment Classifications
Acme Corp classifies employees as follows:
Classification
Definition
Full-time
Regularly scheduled for 40 hours per week
Part-time
Regularly scheduled for 20–39 hours per
week
Temporary
Hired for a defined period not exceeding 12
months
Intern
Enrolled in an academic program; placement
does not exceed 6 months
1


Contractors are not Acme Corp employees and are governed by the terms of their contracting
agreement.
See the Contractor Access Policy for details on contractor system access and
eligibility restrictions.
1.4 Work Hours and Attendance
Standard business hours are 9:00 AM to 6:00 PM local time, Monday through Friday. Flexible
scheduling is available with manager approval, subject to the requirement that employees be
available during core hours of 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM.
Acme Corp supports remote work arrangements. Refer to the Remote Work Policy for eligibility,
in-office requirements, and stipend details.
1.5 Equal Employment Opportunity
Acme Corp provides equal employment opportunities to all employees and applicants regard-
less of race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, veteran status, sexual orienta-
tion, gender identity, or any other characteristic protected by applicable law.
Chapter 2 — Code of Conduct
2.1 Professional Standards
Employees are expected to conduct themselves professionally in all interactions with
colleagues, customers, partners, and vendors. This includes:
• Treating all individuals with respect and dignity.
• Communicating honestly and transparently.
• Protecting confidential information and company assets.
• Complying with all applicable laws, regulations, and company policies.
• Reporting violations of this code or any company policy through the appropriate channels.
2.2 Conflicts of Interest
Employees must avoid situations where personal interests conflict, or appear to conflict, with
the interests of the company. Examples include:
• Outside employment with a competitor, customer, or vendor without written disclosure
to Human Resources.
• Financial interests in companies that do business with Acme Corp, beyond diversified
mutual fund holdings.
• Hiring or supervising family members or close personal relations without disclosure and
approval from the VP of the relevant department.
Conflicts of interest must be disclosed in writing to Human Resources. The Chief Compliance
Officer reviews disclosed conflicts and determines appropriate mitigations.
2.3 Anti-Harassment and Non-Discrimination
Acme Corp maintains a zero-tolerance policy for harassment, discrimination, and retaliation.
All forms of harassment — including verbal, physical, visual, and electronic — are prohibited.
Employees who experience or witness harassment should report it immediately via:
• Their direct manager or any member of management.
2


• Human Resources.
• The anonymous ethics hotline at 1-800-555-0142.
All reports are investigated within 48 hours of receipt. Retaliation against any employee who
reports a concern in good faith is grounds for immediate termination. Refer to the Harassment
Policy and Reporting Procedures document for the full investigation process and resolution
framework.
2.4 Use of Company Resources
Company equipment, systems, and data are provided for business purposes. Limited personal
use is permitted provided it does not interfere with work responsibilities, consume excessive
resources, or violate any company policy. All use of company systems is subject to monitoring
as described in the Employee Monitoring Policy.
Chapter 3 — Compensation and Benefits Overview
3.1 Compensation Structure
Acme Corp uses a leveled compensation framework (L1 through L8) with defined salary bands
for each level. Compensation includes base salary, equity grants (RSUs), and eligibility for
performance-based bonuses. Details on salary ranges and equity grants by level are in the
Compensation Bands document and the RSU Vesting Schedule.
The annual merit cycle occurs in April. Adjustments are based on performance ratings from
the annual review process, market data, and internal equity.
3.2 Health Insurance
Acme Corp offers three health insurance plans:

All full-time employees and their dependents are eligible for health insurance beginning on
the first day of employment. Plan changes may be made during the annual open enrollment
period, which closes on November 15 each year, or within 30 days of a qualifying life event.
3.3 Retirement Benefits
Acme Corp offers a 401(k) plan with employer matching. The company matches 50% of em-
ployee contributions up to 6% of salary, for a maximum employer contribution of 3% of salary.
Employer matching contributions vest over a 4-year schedule. Employees are eligible to par-
ticipate from their first day of employment.
Refer to the 401(k) Matching Policy for full details on contribution limits, vesting, and fund
options.
3


3.4 Health Savings and Flexible Spending Accounts
Employees enrolled in the Basic or Standard health plans may open a Health Savings Account
(HSA). Employees in any plan may open a Flexible Spending Account (FSA) for dependent
care or limited-purpose medical expenses. Contribution limits follow IRS guidelines and are
published annually during open enrollment. Details are in the HSA/FSA Policy.
3.5 Life Insurance
Acme Corp provides basic life insurance coverage at one times annual base salary at no cost
to the employee. Employees may purchase additional coverage up to five times base salary
through payroll deduction. Beneficiary designations are managed through the benefits portal.
See the Life Insurance Benefit document for details.
3.6 Equity Compensation
Eligible employees receive Restricted Stock Unit (RSU) grants as part of their total compensa-
tion package. Grant sizes vary by level. RSU vesting schedules are defined in the RSU Vesting
Schedule. Refresh grants may be awarded during the annual compensation cycle based on
performance and retention considerations.
3.7 Employee Referral Program
Employees who refer candidates that are subsequently hired may receive a referral bonus
of $2,000. The referral form must be submitted before the candidate applies. The referred
candidate must remain employed for at least 90 days for the bonus to be paid. Bonuses are
paid in the next regular pay cycle following the 90-day mark.
Each employee may submit
a maximum of 5 referrals per year. Full terms are in the Employee Referral Bonus Program
document.
3.8 Wellness Program
Acme Corp provides a wellness benefit that includes a gym reimbursement of $50 per month,
an annual health screening, and access to the Employee Assistance Program (EAP) for confi-
dential counseling. See the Wellness Program Overview for details.
3.9 Other Benefits
Additional benefits include:
• Home office stipend: $500 one-time stipend for employees approved for remote work.
• Tuition reimbursement: Up to $5,250 per calendar year for job-related coursework,
available after the probationary period.
• Commuter benefits: Pre-tax transit and parking contributions per IRS limits.
• Employee discounts: Access to negotiated rates on select technology, travel, and well-
ness services through the benefits portal.
Chapter 4 — Leave Policies
4.1 Paid Time Off (Vacation)
Full-time employees accrue vacation based on tenure:
4


Tenure
Annual Vacation Days
Annual Hours
Year 1
15 days
120 hours
Years 2–9
20 days
160 hours
10+ years
25 days
200 hours
Vacation accrues on a per-pay-period basis. Maximum carryover is 40 hours into the following
calendar year. Unused vacation above the carryover limit is forfeited on January 1.
Vacation requests require manager approval. Requests of five or more consecutive days should
be submitted at least two weeks in advance.
4.2 Sick Leave
Full-time employees accrue sick leave at a rate of 1 day per month, up to a maximum balance
of 15 days. Sick leave may be used for the employee’s own illness or injury, medical appoint-
ments, or to care for an immediate family member. A doctor’s note is required for absences
exceeding three consecutive days.
4.3 Jury Duty Leave
Employees receive full pay for up to 10 days of jury duty per year. Employees must notify their
manager and Human Resources promptly upon receiving a jury duty summons and provide a
copy of the summons. Time beyond 10 days is unpaid but job-protected.
4.4 Bereavement Leave
Employees are granted up to 5 paid days of bereavement leave for the death of an immediate
family member (spouse, child, parent, sibling) and up to 3 paid days for extended family (grand-
parent, grandchild, in-law, aunt, uncle). Additional unpaid leave may be granted at manager
discretion.
4.5 Parental Leave
• Birthing parents: 16 weeks of paid leave.
• Non-birthing parents: 8 weeks of paid leave.
Both policies require a minimum of 1 year of continuous employment at the time leave be-
gins. Parental leave runs concurrently with any applicable FMLA leave. Employees must notify
Human Resources at least 30 days before the expected start of leave, or as soon as practicable.
4.6 Family and Medical Leave (FMLA)
Eligible employees may take up to 12 weeks of unpaid, job-protected leave per 12-month
period under the Family and Medical Leave Act. Eligibility requires 12 months of tenure at
Acme Corp.
The FMLA request process is:
1. Notify HR at least 30 days in advance of foreseeable leave, or as soon as practicable for
unforeseeable leave.
2. Submit medical certification using the company’s designated form within 15 calendar
days of the request.
3. HR reviews the request and responds within 5 business days with an approval, denial,
or request for additional information.
5


4. Manager adjusts workload to accommodate the leave period, including reassigning
responsibilities and updating project timelines.
Refer to the FMLA Leave Procedure document for the complete process, intermittent leave
provisions, and return-to-work requirements.
4.7 Short-Term Disability
Employees who cannot work due to a qualifying medical condition may receive short-term
disability benefits: 60% of base salary for up to 12 weeks, following a 7-day waiting period.
STD benefits coordinate with any applicable FMLA leave. See the Short-Term Disability Policy
for eligibility, claims process, and benefit calculations.
4.8 Other Leave
• Voting leave: Up to 2 hours of paid time to vote on election days, where not otherwise
provided by state law.
• Military leave: As required by USERRA. Job-protected with benefits continuation.
• Personal leave: Unpaid leave of up to 30 days may be granted at the discretion of
the employee’s VP and Human Resources for circumstances not covered by other leave
policies.
Chapter 5 — Workplace Policies
5.1 Remote Work
Acme Corp supports hybrid work arrangements. Remote work requires manager approval. Ap-
proved remote employees must:
• Work from the office a minimum of 2 days per week.
• Be available during core hours of 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM in their local time zone.
• Maintain a workspace that meets the requirements outlined in the Laptop Security Re-
quirements.
A one-time home office setup stipend of $500 is provided upon approval. The Remote Work
Policy was last updated on September 12, 2025, and contains the full terms and conditions.
5.2 Expense Reimbursement
Employees may submit expense reports for business-related costs. Approval thresholds are:
Amount
Required Approval
Under $500
Direct manager
$500 – $10,000
Direct manager and department head
Over $10,000
Direct manager, department head, and CFO
Expenses must be submitted within 30 days of being incurred. Receipts are required for all
expenses over $25. Refer to the Expense Report Approval Policy for reimbursement timelines
and eligible expense categories.
6


5.3 Performance Reviews
Acme Corp uses a continuous feedback model supported by a formal review cycle:
• Quarterly check-ins: Structured 1:1 conversation between employee and manager to
review goals, provide feedback, and discuss development.
• Annual 360-degree review: Conducted in Q4. Peers, direct reports (where applicable),
and cross-functional collaborators submit feedback.
• Self-assessment: Employees complete a written self-assessment covering accomplish-
ments, areas for growth, and career goals.
• Manager assessment: Managers prepare an independent written evaluation of the em-
ployee’s performance.
• Calibration meeting:
Department leaders and Human Resources review all ratings
within a department to ensure consistency.
• Final rating: The calibrated rating is shared with the employee during the review meet-
ing, along with any compensation implications.
Ratings inform the annual merit cycle (April), promotion decisions, and performance-based
bonuses. Detailed procedures are in the Performance Review Process document.
5.4 Open Enrollment
The annual benefits open enrollment period runs from November 1 through November 15. Dur-
ing this window, employees may:
• Change health plan elections.
• Add or remove dependents.
• Adjust life insurance coverage.
• Modify HSA or FSA contributions.
Elections made during open enrollment take effect January 1 of the following year. Outside of
open enrollment, changes are permitted only within 30 days of a qualifying life event (marriage,
birth, adoption, loss of other coverage).
5.5 Data Classification and Handling
All employees are responsible for handling company data according to its classification level.
Acme Corp uses four tiers:
• Public: Information intended for external distribution (marketing materials, published
APIs).
• Internal: Information for employee use that is not sensitive (internal announcements,
team wiki pages).
• Confidential: Sensitive business information (financial data, customer lists, strategic
plans). Access restricted to employees with a business need.
• Restricted: Highly sensitive information (PII, PHI, credentials, security audit results). Ac-
cess controlled by the Data Classification Policy and subject to encryption and audit log-
ging requirements.
Employees must complete annual security awareness training. Failure to comply with data
handling requirements may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.
7


Chapter 6 — Separation
6.1 Voluntary Resignation
Employees who wish to resign should provide at least two weeks’ written notice to their man-
ager and Human Resources. An exit interview will be conducted by Human Resources during
the notice period.
6.2 Involuntary Termination
Involuntary termination may result from performance issues (following the performance im-
provement process), policy violations, or business reasons such as position elimination. The
termination process is documented in the Termination Procedures document.
6.3 Final Pay and Benefits
Final paychecks are issued in accordance with applicable state law. Accrued, unused vacation is
paid out at the employee’s current rate. Employees who are terminated or resign are eligible for
COBRA continuation of health benefits as described in the Termination Procedures document.
6.4 Return of Company Property
All company property — including laptops, access badges, mobile devices, and any documents
or data (physical or electronic) — must be returned on or before the employee’s last day.

Employee Monitoring Policy
Employee Monitoring Policy
Document ID: ITSEC-008 Effective Date: May 1, 2024 Approved by: VP of Engineering,
Chief Compliance Officer Classification: Confidential
1. Purpose
This policy describes the scope, methods, and governance of electronic monitoring conducted
by Acme Corp on company-owned systems, networks, and devices. Monitoring is performed to
protect company and customer data, detect security threats, ensure compliance with company
policies, and meet regulatory obligations.
2. Scope
This policy applies to all Acme Corp employees using company-provided systems and devices.
Contractors and temporary workers are subject to the same monitoring when accessing Acme
Corp systems under the Contractor Access Policy.
Monitoring applies to activity on:
• Company-issued laptops and workstations.
• Company email (all messages sent, received, and drafted via @acmecorp.com accounts).
• Company messaging platforms (Slack, Microsoft Teams).
• Company network traffic (corporate and VPN connections).
• Company-managed cloud applications (Google Workspace, Jira, Confluence, GitHub).
• Personal devices enrolled in the MDM program (limited to managed application data only;
see the BYOD Policy for privacy boundaries).
3. Monitoring Methods
3.1 Email Monitoring
All email sent to or from @acmecorp.com addresses is subject to automated scanning for:
• Malware and phishing detection.
• Data Loss Prevention (DLP) rule violations (e.g., Restricted data sent to external recipients
without encryption).
• Regulatory compliance keywords (for employees in regulated functions).
Email content is not routinely reviewed by humans. Automated DLP alerts are reviewed by the
Information Security team only when a rule violation is triggered.
3.2 Web and Network Monitoring
Network traffic is monitored for:
1


• Bandwidth usage by category (streaming, social media, cloud storage).
• Connections to known malicious domains or IP addresses.
• Data exfiltration indicators (large outbound transfers, unusual upload patterns).
• Protocol anomalies (non-standard ports, encrypted tunnels to unauthorized endpoints).
Web browsing history on company devices is logged by the endpoint agent. Logs are retained
for security investigation purposes and are not used for general performance evaluation.
3.3 Endpoint Activity Monitoring
The endpoint detection and response (EDR) agent on company devices monitors:
• Process execution (to detect malware, unauthorized software, and privilege escalation).
• File access patterns on local storage and network drives.
• Peripheral device connections (USB drives, external storage).
• Application usage metadata (which applications are running, for how long).
3.4 Keystroke Logging
Keystroke logging is not conducted on a routine or company-wide basis. Keystroke monitoring
may be activated only under the following conditions:
• A documented, active security investigation approved by the VP of Engineering or the
Chief Compliance Officer.
• Scope limited to the specific user account and time period relevant to the investigation.
• Notification to Human Resources before activation.
Keystroke data collected during an investigation is retained only for the duration of the inves-
tigation plus 90 days, then permanently deleted.
4. Disclosure
4.1 Employee Notice
All employees are informed of monitoring practices through:
• This policy, which is distributed during onboarding and available on the company wiki.
• The Employee Handbook, Section 2.4 (Use of Company Resources).
• The Acceptable Use Policy, Section 4 (Monitoring Disclosure).
• A login banner displayed when connecting to company systems: “This system is moni-
tored. By logging in, you consent to monitoring in accordance with the Employee Moni-
toring Policy.”
4.2 Transparency Principle
Acme Corp does not conduct covert monitoring except during authorized security investiga-
tions (see Section 3.4). Employees have access to this policy at all times and may request
a summary of the categories of data collected about them by contacting the Data Protection
Officer.
5. Supervisor Access to Monitoring Data
Managers and supervisors do not have direct access to monitoring data. Access is restricted
to:
2


Role
Access Level
Information Security team
Full access for security investigations and
DLP alert review
Data Protection Officer
Audit access for compliance reviews
Human Resources
Access to specific records upon documented
request tied to an active HR investigation
Legal
Access upon documented request tied to
litigation hold or regulatory inquiry
5.1 Supervisor Request Process
If a supervisor believes that monitoring data is needed for a performance or conduct concern:
1. The supervisor submits a written request to Human Resources describing the specific
concern and the data sought.
2. Human Resources reviews the request with Legal to ensure it is proportionate and lawful.
3. If approved, the Information Security team provides the relevant data to HR, not directly
to the supervisor.
4. HR shares findings with the supervisor on a need-to-know basis.
Blanket requests (“give me all of this employee’s browsing history”) are not approved. Re-
quests must be tied to a specific, documented concern.
6. Data Retention
Data Type
Retention Period
Email DLP alerts
2 years
Network traffic logs
1 year
Endpoint activity logs
1 year (hot), 6 years (cold)
Web browsing logs
6 months
Keystroke investigation data
Investigation duration + 90 days
Retention periods align with the Audit Log Retention policy and regulatory requirements for
SOC 2 and HIPAA.
7. Automated Decision-Making
Acme Corp does not use monitoring data as the sole basis for automated decisions that produce
legal or similarly significant effects on employees. Specifically:
• No employee is terminated, disciplined, demoted, or denied a benefit based solely on an
automated monitoring alert or score.
• All DLP alerts and anomaly detections that may lead to an adverse action are reviewed
by a human (Information Security analyst or HR) before any action is taken.
• Employees have the right to contest any adverse action and to request human review of
the monitoring data that informed the action.
This approach aligns with the principles of GDPR Article 22, which provides individuals the right
not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal effects.
While GDPR Article 22 applies specifically to EU data subjects, Acme Corp extends this principle
to all employees globally as a matter of company policy.
3


8. Employee Rights
Employees may:
• Request a summary of the categories of monitoring data collected about them.
• Request correction of inaccurate monitoring records.
• Contest any adverse employment action that was informed by monitoring data.
• Raise concerns about monitoring practices to the Data Protection Officer or the anony-
mous ethics hotline (1-800-555-0142).
9. Policy Governance
This policy is reviewed annually by the Information Security team, Human Resources, and Legal.
The Chief Compliance Officer (Jordan Patel) approves all updates. Questions should be directed
to security@acmecorp.com or the Data Protection Officer.

# Employee Onboarding Checklist

**Effective Date:** May 1, 2024
**Applies to:** All new Acme Corp employees and their managers

## First Day

- [ ] Badge and building access provisioned by Facilities.
- [ ] Laptop and peripherals issued by IT (see Laptop Security Requirements).
- [ ] HR orientation session: company overview, benefits enrollment, policy acknowledgment.
- [ ] Manager welcome meeting: team introductions, initial project overview, and first-week expectations.
- [ ] Complete required compliance training modules (harassment prevention, security awareness, data handling).
- [ ] Enroll in benefits within 30 days via the benefits portal (default: Basic health plan if no selection is made).
- [ ] Set up direct deposit and emergency contact information in the HR system.

## 30-Day Milestone

- [ ] Complete all assigned onboarding training modules in the learning management system.
- [ ] Manager conducts a 30-day check-in to discuss initial impressions, early feedback, and any support needs.
- [ ] Employee completes a 30-day self-reflection (informal, shared with manager).
- [ ] Access to all required systems and tools confirmed by IT and manager.
- [ ] Introduction to cross-functional stakeholders and key collaborators completed.

## 60-Day Milestone

- [ ] Manager conducts a 60-day check-in to assess progress on initial objectives and provide feedback.
- [ ] Employee identifies one development goal for the remainder of the probationary period.
- [ ] Any concerns about fit, performance, or expectations are documented by the manager and shared with HR.

## 90-Day Probationary Review

All new hires enter a 90-day probationary period. The probationary review is a formal evaluation that determines whether the employee transitions to regular employment status.

- [ ] Manager completes the probationary review form (available in the HR system) by the employee's 85th day.
- [ ] Manager and employee hold a probationary review meeting before day 90.
- [ ] Review covers: performance against initial objectives, cultural fit, collaboration feedback from peers, and any areas of concern.
- [ ] HR reviews the completed form and confirms the outcome:
  - **Pass:** Employee transitions to regular status. Eligible for internal transfers, tuition reimbursement, and the employee referral bonus program.
  - **Extended probation:** An additional 30 days with specific improvement targets. Requires HR approval.
  - **Separation:** Employment ends. Coordinated through HR per the Termination Procedures.

## Post-Probation

- [ ] Employee is added to the quarterly check-in schedule per the Performance Review Process.
- [ ] Manager sets annual objectives aligned with team and department goals.
- [ ] Employee gains access to the tuition reimbursement program and employee referral bonus program.
title: Employee Onboarding Checklist

# Employee Referral Bonus Program

**Effective Date:** January 15, 2024
**Applies to:** All full-time Acme Corp employees who have completed their 90-day probationary period

## 1. Overview

Acme Corp encourages employees to refer qualified candidates for open positions. Successful referrals that result in a hire are eligible for a $2,000 referral bonus.

## 2. Eligibility Requirements

To qualify for the referral bonus, all of the following conditions must be met:

1. **Referral form submitted before the candidate applies.** The referring employee must submit a referral through the HR portal or by emailing recruiting@acmecorp.com before the candidate submits their application. Retroactive referrals (submitted after the candidate has already applied) are not eligible.
2. **The candidate is hired** into a full-time position at Acme Corp.
3. **The candidate must remain employed for at least 90 days** from their start date. If the candidate voluntarily resigns or is terminated for cause before the 90-day mark, the bonus is forfeited.
4. **The referring employee must be employed at Acme Corp** at the time the bonus is paid. Employees who resign or are terminated before the bonus payment date forfeit the bonus.

## 3. Bonus Payment

The $2,000 referral bonus is paid in the next regular pay cycle following the referred candidate's 90th day of employment. The bonus is subject to standard payroll taxes and withholding.

## 4. Referral Limits

Each employee may submit a maximum of 5 referrals per calendar year. This limit applies to referrals submitted, not hires made. If an employee has submitted 5 referral forms in a given year and none resulted in a hire, no additional referrals may be submitted until the following January 1.

## 5. Exclusions

The following are not eligible for referral bonuses:

- Referrals made by members of the hiring team for the specific role (hiring manager, interviewers on the loop, recruiting team).
- Referrals of former Acme Corp employees who separated within the prior 12 months.
- Referrals for temporary, intern, or contractor positions.
- Referrals submitted by employees still in their 90-day probationary period.

## 6. Multiple Referrals for the Same Candidate

If more than one employee refers the same candidate, the bonus is awarded to the employee whose referral was submitted first, based on the timestamp in the HR system. Duplicate referrals are not split.

## 7. Policy Governance

This program is administered by the Talent Acquisition team within Human Resources. Questions should be directed to recruiting@acmecorp.com.
title: Employee Referral Bonus Program

Encryption Standards
Encryption Standards
Document ID: ITSEC-004 Effective Date: August 15, 2024 Approved by: VP of Engineering
Classification: Confidential
1. Purpose
This document defines the encryption requirements for all data processed, stored, and trans-
mitted by Acme Corp. These standards are part of Acme Corp’s technical safeguards for protect-
ing sensitive information, including electronic protected health information (ePHI) processed
on behalf of healthcare customers.
2. Data in Transit
2.1 Minimum Standards
All data in transit must be encrypted using TLS 1.3. TLS 1.2 is permitted only for legacy inte-
grations where TLS 1.3 is not supported by the counterparty, and only with approved cipher
suites. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are prohibited on all Acme Corp systems.
Internal service-to-service communication within the production zone must use mutual TLS
(mTLS) to authenticate both endpoints.
2.2 Approved Cipher Suites
Protocol
Approved Cipher Suites
TLS 1.3
TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384,
TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256,
TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
TLS 1.2 (legacy only)
ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384,
ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256
Cipher suites using CBC mode, RC4, 3DES, or static RSA key exchange are prohibited.
2.3 Certificate Management
• All public-facing endpoints use certificates issued by a trusted Certificate Authority.
• Internal services use certificates from Acme Corp’s internal CA, managed through the
automated certificate provisioning system.
• Certificates must use RSA 2048-bit keys (minimum) or ECDSA P-256 keys.
• Certificate expiration is monitored automatically. Certificates must be renewed at least
30 days before expiration.
1


3. Data at Rest
3.1 General Requirements
All Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted at rest using AES-256 or an equivalent
approved algorithm. This includes:
• Database storage: transparent data encryption (TDE) or application-level encryption.
• Object storage: server-side encryption with customer-managed keys (SSE-CMK).
• File systems: encrypted volumes (LUKS on Linux, FileVault on macOS, BitLocker on Win-
dows).
• Backup media: encrypted before transfer to offsite or cold storage.
3.2 Laptop and Endpoint Encryption
All company-managed laptops and workstations must have full disk encryption enabled. See
the Laptop Security Requirements for enforcement details.
4. ePHI Encryption Requirements
Acme Corp processes electronic protected health information (ePHI) on behalf of healthcare
customers under executed Business Associate Agreements. The following technical safeguards
apply to all systems that store, process, or transmit ePHI:
4.1 Technical Safeguards for ePHI
• Encryption at rest: ePHI must be encrypted using AES-256. Application-level encryption
is required in addition to storage-level encryption (defense in depth).
• Encryption in transit: ePHI must be transmitted exclusively over TLS 1.3. No exceptions
for legacy protocols.
• Access controls: ePHI access requires role-based access controls, multi-factor authen-
tication, and audit logging.
See the Access Control Policy for the access provisioning
process.
• Key isolation: Encryption keys for ePHI must be stored separately from keys used for
non-ePHI data. Key access is restricted to the ePHI system owner and the InfoSec key
management team.
• Audit trail: All access to ePHI (read, write, export) is logged to an immutable audit trail,
retained per the Audit Log Retention policy.
4.2 HIPAA Alignment
These technical safeguards satisfy the encryption requirements specified in the HIPAA Security
Rule, including the addressable implementation specification for encryption under 45 CFR §
164.312(a)(2)(iv) and the transmission security standard under 45 CFR § 164.312(e)(1).
Acme Corp treats encryption as a required implementation, not addressable, for all systems
processing ePHI. This exceeds the HIPAA minimum but reflects our risk assessment and the
sensitivity of the data entrusted to us by healthcare customers.
5. Key Management
5.1 Key Storage
All encryption keys are stored in HashiCorp Vault. Keys must not be stored in source code,
configuration files, environment variables, or any location accessible to application code at
2


rest.
5.2 Key Rotation Schedule
Key Type
Rotation Frequency
Data encryption keys (DEKs)
Every 365 days
Key encryption keys (KEKs)
Every 730 days
TLS certificates
Every 365 days (automated)
API keys and service account secrets
Every 180 days
ePHI encryption keys
Every 365 days (separate rotation from
non-ePHI keys)
Keys must be rotated immediately if compromise is suspected, regardless of the scheduled
rotation date.
5.3 Key Destruction
Retired keys are retained in a disabled state for 90 days to support decryption of data encrypted
under the old key during the transition period. After 90 days, retired keys are permanently
destroyed using a cryptographic erasure process.
6. Exceptions
Systems that cannot meet these encryption standards (e.g., legacy hardware, third-party inte-
grations with fixed protocol support) must document the gap in a Security Exception Request
with compensating controls. Exceptions are approved by the InfoSec team lead and reviewed
every 90 days.
7. Policy Governance
This document is reviewed annually by the Information Security team. Questions should be
directed to security@acmecorp.com.
Version 3.0 — Last revised August 15, 2024
