# Engineering Hiring Rubric

**Effective Date:** August 1, 2024
**Owner:** Engineering Leadership + Talent Acquisition
**Classification:** Confidential — do not share with candidates

---

## 1. Interview Structure

All engineering candidates go through a standardized interview loop:

| Stage | Duration | Interviewer | Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| Recruiter screen | 30 min | Talent Acquisition | Role fit, compensation expectations, timeline |
| Hiring manager screen | 45 min | Hiring manager | Background deep-dive, team fit, motivation |
| Technical interview 1 | 60 min | Engineer (L3+) | Coding and problem-solving |
| Technical interview 2 | 60 min | Engineer (L3+) | System design (L3+ candidates) or data structures (L1–L2) |
| Cross-functional interview | 45 min | Product Manager or Designer | Collaboration, communication, product thinking |
| Values interview | 30 min | Any interviewer trained on values rubric | Alignment with Acme Corp engineering values |

L6+ candidates add an architecture review session (90 min) with the VP of Engineering or a Distinguished Engineer.

## 2. Evaluation Criteria

Each interviewer scores the candidate on a 4-point scale:

| Score | Label | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | Strong Hire | Clearly exceeds the bar for the level. Demonstrated exceptional skill in the focus area. |
| 3 | Hire | Meets the bar for the level. Solid performance with minor gaps that can be developed on the job. |
| 2 | Lean No Hire | Below the bar in meaningful ways. Significant concerns that coaching alone may not resolve. |
| 1 | No Hire | Well below the bar. Critical gaps in required competencies. |

## 3. Level Expectations

### L1 — Associate / Junior

- Solves well-scoped coding problems with standard data structures and algorithms.
- Writes clean, readable code with guidance.
- Communicates thought process clearly during the interview.
- No system design expectation.

### L2 — Mid-Level

- Solves moderately complex coding problems independently, including edge cases.
- Discusses tradeoffs in implementation choices (time vs space, readability vs performance).
- Demonstrates familiarity with one or more production-relevant technologies (databases, APIs, cloud services).
- Basic system design: can design a simple service with a database, API, and caching layer.

### L3 — Senior

- Solves complex coding problems efficiently and handles ambiguity well.
- System design: can design a multi-service system with appropriate data storage, caching, queueing, and failure handling.
- Demonstrates ownership mindset: considers testing, observability, and operational concerns.
- Evidence of mentoring or technical leadership.

### L4 — Staff

- All L3 expectations, plus:
- System design at scale: can reason about scalability, consistency tradeoffs, and multi-region architectures.
- Demonstrated impact across team boundaries.
- Evidence of driving technical strategy or leading a significant project end-to-end.

### L5+ — Senior Staff and Above

- All L4 expectations, plus:
- Demonstrates industry-caliber depth in one or more technical domains.
- Evidence of influencing engineering direction at the organizational level.
- Architecture review evaluates ability to assess tradeoffs across multiple systems and articulate long-term technical vision.

## 4. Calibration

After all interviews are complete:

1. The recruiter collects scorecards from all interviewers within 24 hours.
2. The hiring manager convenes a debrief meeting (30 min) with all interviewers.
3. Each interviewer shares their score and rationale. Discussion focuses on disagreements (2+ point spread between interviewers).
4. The hiring manager makes the final hire/no-hire decision. The decision is documented with rationale.
5. If the decision is borderline, the hiring manager may request one additional interview (different interviewer, different focus area).

### Calibration Rules

- A single "No Hire" (score 1) from any interviewer triggers a mandatory debrief discussion. The hiring manager must specifically address the concern before proceeding.
- Two or more "Lean No Hire" (score 2) across different focus areas is a strong signal against proceeding. The hiring manager must document specific reasons for overriding this signal.
- Interviewers must not change their score based on peer pressure during the debrief. If new information changes their assessment, they document the reason for the change.

## 5. Anti-Bias Practices

- Interview questions are drawn from a question bank maintained by the Engineering Leadership team. Interviewers may not use ad-hoc questions without prior approval.
- All interviewers complete the Interviewing and Bias Awareness training annually per the Mandatory Training Catalog.
- Candidate evaluation must reference only job-relevant criteria. Personal attributes (school attended, previous employer prestige, hobbies, family status) must not factor into the assessment.
- Scorecards are submitted independently before the debrief meeting to prevent anchoring.

## 6. Policy Governance

This rubric is maintained by the Engineering Leadership team in coordination with Talent Acquisition. Updates are reviewed semi-annually. Questions: eng-hiring@acmecorp.com.

---

*Version 2.0 — Last revised August 1, 2024*
title: Engineering Hiring Rubric

text:
# Engineering Standards

**Effective Date:** January 15, 2025
**Owner:** Sarah Chen, VP of Engineering
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp engineering teams

---

## 1. Language Choices

Acme Corp uses three primary languages across its stack. Team leads may not introduce additional languages without VP of Engineering approval.

| Language | Usage | Version Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Python | API services, ML pipeline, data processing, internal tooling | 3.11+ (track latest stable minus one) |
| Go | High-throughput ingestion service, real-time event bus, CLI tools | 1.22+ (track latest stable) |
| TypeScript | Frontend (React), serverless functions, BFF (backend-for-frontend) layer | 5.3+ (track latest stable) |

SQL (PostgreSQL dialect) is used for database queries and migrations but is not counted as a primary language.

Shell scripts (Bash) are permitted for CI/CD pipelines and operational automation. Scripts exceeding 100 lines should be rewritten in Python.

## 2. Code Style

### 2.1 Formatting

All code must be auto-formatted before commit:

| Language | Formatter | Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| Python | `ruff format` | `pyproject.toml` (repo root) |
| Go | `gofmt` | Default settings |
| TypeScript | `prettier` | `.prettierrc` (repo root) |

CI rejects pull requests with formatting violations. No exceptions.

### 2.2 Linting

| Language | Linter | Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| Python | `ruff check` | `pyproject.toml` |
| Go | `golangci-lint` | `.golangci.yml` |
| TypeScript | `eslint` | `.eslintrc.json` |

All lint rules are enforced in CI. Adding a lint suppression comment (`# noqa`, `//nolint`, `// eslint-disable`) requires a justifying comment explaining why the rule does not apply.

### 2.3 Naming Conventions

- Python: `snake_case` for functions and variables, `PascalCase` for classes, `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` for constants.
- Go: Exported identifiers `PascalCase`, unexported `camelCase`, per Go convention.
- TypeScript: `camelCase` for variables and functions, `PascalCase` for types, interfaces, and React components.
- Database: `snake_case` for tables and columns. Table names are plural (`users`, `records`, `audit_logs`).

## 3. Testing Requirements

### 3.1 Coverage Targets

| Level | Minimum Coverage | Enforcement |
|---|---|---|
| Unit tests | 80% line coverage | CI blocks merge below threshold |
| Integration tests | Critical paths covered (no numeric target) | Reviewed in PR by code owner |
| End-to-end tests | Smoke suite must pass on every deploy | CI blocks deploy on failure |

### 3.2 Test Conventions

- Test files live alongside the code they test (`foo.py` → `foo_test.py`, `bar.go` → `bar_test.go`, `baz.ts` → `baz.test.ts`).
- Tests are deterministic. No reliance on wall-clock time, network calls to external services, or shared mutable state between test cases.
- Flaky tests are quarantined in CI within 24 hours of detection and tracked in the engineering backlog. A test that fails intermittently more than twice in a week is quarantined.
- Integration tests that require a database use a dedicated test database instance, reset between test suites.

### 3.3 Load and Performance Tests

Services with an SLO must have a performance test suite that runs weekly in the staging environment. The suite validates that the service meets its latency SLO under expected peak load (2x average daily traffic).

## 4. SLIs/SLOs for Services

Every production service must define at least three Service Level Indicators (SLIs) and corresponding Service Level Objectives (SLOs):

| SLI | Definition | Standard SLO |
|---|---|---|
| Availability | Percentage of successful requests (non-5xx) out of total requests | 99.95% |
| Latency (p99) | 99th percentile response time measured at the load balancer | < 500ms |
| Error rate | Percentage of requests returning a 5xx status code | < 0.05% |

### 4.1 SLO Review

SLOs are reviewed quarterly by the service owner and the engineering manager. If a service misses its SLO in any calendar month, the team must:

1. Conduct a root cause analysis within 5 business days.
2. Identify remediation actions with owners and deadlines.
3. Report findings in the monthly engineering review.

### 4.2 Error Budgets

Each service has an error budget derived from its availability SLO. For a 99.95% SLO, the monthly error budget is 21.6 minutes of downtime (in a 30-day month). When the error budget is exhausted:

- Feature development freezes for the affected service.
- The team focuses exclusively on reliability work until the budget is replenished at the start of the next month.
- The VP of Engineering may grant an exception for critical business deadlines, documented in writing.

## 5. Dependency Management

### 5.1 Version Pinning

All dependencies are version-pinned in lock files (`poetry.lock`, `go.sum`, `package-lock.json`). Floating version ranges in manifest files are prohibited in production services.

### 5.2 Automated Updates

Dependabot (GitHub) is configured to open pull requests for dependency updates weekly. Security updates are prioritized per the Vulnerability Management Policy:

- Critical CVEs: PR merged within 7 days.
- High CVEs: PR merged within 30 days.
- Routine updates: Batched and merged monthly.

### 5.3 Approved and Prohibited Dependencies

The engineering wiki maintains a list of pre-approved libraries for common tasks (HTTP clients, logging, serialization, testing frameworks). Libraries not on the approved list require a lightweight review by the relevant platform team before adoption.

Prohibited categories:
- Libraries with known unpatched critical vulnerabilities.
- Libraries with licenses incompatible with Acme Corp's commercial use (GPL, AGPL without a commercial exception).
- Libraries that have not been updated in 2+ years (abandoned projects).

## 6. Code Review

All production code changes require at least one approving review from a code owner before merge. See the Code Review Guidelines for detailed expectations, approval rules, and merge queue procedures.

## 7. Documentation

Every service must maintain:

- A `README.md` with setup instructions, architecture overview, and deployment notes.
- An API contract (OpenAPI spec for HTTP services, protobuf definitions for gRPC services).
- A runbook linked from the service catalog, covering common operational tasks and known failure modes.

## 8. Policy Governance

These standards are maintained by the Engineering Platform team and approved by the VP of Engineering (Sarah Chen). Proposed changes are discussed in the biweekly engineering standards review meeting. Questions should be directed to eng-standards@acmecorp.com.

Enterprise Refund Policy
Enterprise Refund Policy
Effective Date: January 1, 2024 Applies to: All Acme Corp enterprise customers with an
executed Enterprise Agreement
1. Scope
This policy governs refund requests from enterprise customers (customers with an annual con-
tract value of $25,000 or more). Consumer and self-serve customers are subject to the sepa-
rate Consumer Refund Policy.
2. Eligibility
Enterprise customers are eligible for a refund under the following conditions:
• The customer has an active Enterprise Agreement with Acme Corp.
• The refund request relates to subscription fees, not professional services (professional
services refunds are handled under the terms of the applicable Statement of Work).
• The customer has raised any service concerns with their Customer Success Manager prior
to requesting a refund.
3. Refund Types
3.1 Cancellation Within 45 Days
Enterprise customers who cancel their subscription within 45 days of the initial purchase date
are eligible for a full refund of all fees paid. No proration or deductions apply.
The 45-day window is measured from the date the Enterprise Agreement is executed, not from
the date of first use or provisioning.
3.2 Cancellation After 45 Days
After the 45-day window, refunds are prorated based on the remaining contract term. The
prorated refund is calculated as:
Prorated Refund = (Remaining Full Months / Total Contract Months) × Annual Fee Paid
Partial months are not included in the remaining term calculation. For example, a customer
who cancels 7 months and 15 days into a 12-month contract receives a prorated refund for 4
remaining full months (months 9–12).
1


3.3 Service Credit Conversion
Customers who have accumulated service credits under the Enterprise SLA may not convert
service credits to a cash refund. Service credits are applied to future invoices only.
4. Refund Process
4.1 Request Submission
Refund requests must be submitted through the enterprise support portal at support.acmecorp.com
or by emailing enterprise-support@acmecorp.com. The request must include:
• Customer name and account ID.
• Enterprise Agreement reference number.
• Reason for the refund request.
• Whether the customer is requesting full cancellation or a partial refund.
4.2 Enterprise Refund Clause
This section constitutes the canonical enterprise refund clause referenced in Acme Corp’s En-
terprise Agreement (Schedule B, Section 4.2):
Enterprise customers may request a full refund within 45 days of purchase. After
45 days, refunds are prorated based on remaining contract term. Refund requests
must be submitted through the enterprise support portal and are processed within
10 business days.
4.3 Processing Timeline
All refund requests are processed within 10 business days of receipt. Processing includes:
1. The Customer Success Manager acknowledges the request within 1 business day.
2. Finance verifies the contract terms, payment history, and eligibility.
3. If approved, Finance issues the refund via the original payment method (wire transfer or
ACH).
4. The customer receives a confirmation email with the refund amount and expected deposit
date.
Refund processing begins after the customer confirms their cancellation intent. Submitting a
refund request does not automatically cancel the subscription; the customer must separately
confirm cancellation.
4.4 Approval Authority
Refund Amount
Required Approval
Under $25,000
VP of Customer Success
$25,000 – $100,000
VP of Customer Success and CFO
Over $100,000
VP of Customer Success, CFO, and CEO
5. Non-Refundable Items
The following are not eligible for refund:
• Professional services fees for work already performed.
2


• One-time setup or onboarding fees, once the onboarding process has begun.
• Third-party pass-through costs (e.g., SMS delivery, cloud infrastructure charges billed at
cost).
• Fees for usage-based overages that have already been consumed.
6. Contract Termination
A refund does not automatically terminate the Enterprise Agreement.
The customer must
follow the termination process specified in Section 7 of the Enterprise SLA. Data retention and
deletion upon termination follow the Customer Deletion Workflow.
7. Policy Governance
This policy is administered by the Finance department in coordination with Customer Success.
Questions should be directed to finance@acmecorp.com.

Acme Corp Enterprise Service Level Agreement
Acme Corp Enterprise Service Level Agreement
Agreement Version: 4.0 Effective Date: June 1, 2024 Applies to: All Acme Corp enterprise
customers with an executed Enterprise Agreement
1. Service Availability
1.1 Uptime Guarantee
Acme Corp guarantees 99.95% uptime for all API endpoints covered under this SLA, measured
on a calendar-month basis. Uptime is calculated as:
Uptime % = ((Total Minutes in Month −Downtime Minutes) / Total Minutes in Month) × 100
A month with 43,200 total minutes (30 days) permits a maximum of 21.6 minutes of downtime
before the guarantee is breached.
1.2 Covered Services
The uptime guarantee applies to:
• All production REST API endpoints (api.acmecorp.com).
• The web application (app.acmecorp.com).
• The webhook delivery system.
• The authentication and SSO service.
1.3 Excluded from Uptime Calculation
The following are not counted as downtime:
• Scheduled maintenance windows (communicated at least 72 hours in advance, limited to
4 hours per month).
• Degradation caused by the customer’s own systems, network, or configuration.
• Force majeure events (natural disasters, government actions, widespread internet out-
ages).
• Sandbox and staging environments.
• Features in beta or early access, unless specifically included by written amendment.
2. Service Credits
If Acme Corp fails to meet the 99.95% uptime guarantee in a given calendar month, the cus-
tomer is entitled to service credits as follows:
1


Monthly Uptime
Service Credit (% of Monthly Fee)
99.90% – 99.95%
10%
99.00% – 99.89%
25%
Below 99.00%
50%
2.1 Credit Request Process
• Credits are not applied automatically. The customer must submit a credit request within
30 days of the end of the affected month.
• Requests are submitted to the customer’s account manager or to sla-credits@acmecorp.com.
• Acme Corp will review the request and respond within 10 business days.
• If approved, credits are applied to the next invoice. Credits are not redeemable for cash
and do not carry over beyond 12 months.
2.2 Credit Cap
Total service credits in any 12-month period shall not exceed 30% of the customer’s annual
contract value. This cap does not limit the customer’s right to terminate the agreement under
Section 7.
3. Support Tiers
Enterprise customers receive support according to the following response time commitments:
Severity
Description
Initial Response
Update

Resolution

SEV1 — Critical

service outage

3.1 Support Channels
Enterprise customers have access to the following support channels:
2


• Support portal: support.acmecorp.com (24/7 ticket submission).
• Email: enterprise-support@acmecorp.com.
• Phone: Dedicated support line for SEV1 and SEV2 issues, available 24/7.
• Slack Connect: Direct channel with the Acme Corp support engineering team (provi-
sioned during onboarding).
3.2 Dedicated Account Team
Each enterprise customer is assigned:
• A Customer Success Manager (CSM) responsible for business reviews, adoption, and
escalations.
• A Technical Account Manager (TAM) available for architecture reviews, integration guid-
ance, and proactive health checks. TAM allocation varies by contract tier.
3.3 Escalation Path
If a support issue is not being resolved within the committed timeline, the customer may esca-
late:
1. Level 1: Customer Success Manager.
2. Level 2: VP of Customer Success.
3. Level 3: VP of Engineering (Sarah Chen).
Escalation contact details are provided during customer onboarding and updated annually.
4. Performance Standards
4.1 API Latency
Acme Corp targets the following API latency performance for production endpoints:
Metric
Target
Median (p50) response time
< 100ms
95th percentile (p95) response time
< 250ms
99th percentile (p99) response time
< 500ms
Latency targets are measured at the Acme Corp load balancer and do not include network
transit time between the customer and Acme Corp’s infrastructure.
Latency is reported in the customer’s monthly performance dashboard.
Persistent latency
above target levels is treated as a SEV3 support issue.
4.2 Data Durability
Customer data stored in the Acme Corp platform has a durability target of 99.999999999%
(eleven nines). This is achieved through:
• Synchronous replication across multiple availability zones.
• Daily full backups with 90-day retention.
• Continuous write-ahead log shipping with a recovery point objective (RPO) of 1 hour.
3


4.3 Rate Limits
API rate limits for enterprise customers:
Endpoint Category
Rate Limit
Read operations
1,000 requests per second per customer
Write operations
200 requests per second per customer
Bulk export
10 concurrent requests per customer
Search / query
500 requests per second per customer
Customers who require higher limits may request a rate limit increase through their TAM. In-
creases are evaluated based on infrastructure capacity and may require a contract amend-
ment.
5. Data Handling
5.1 Data Residency
By default, enterprise customer data is stored in the US-East region. Customers requiring EU
data residency may request deployment to the EU-West region at no additional cost. Data
residency region is specified in the customer’s Order Form.
5.2 Data Encryption
All customer data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3).
Customers may
provide their own encryption keys (BYOK) for data at rest upon request. See the Encryption
Standards document for details.
5.3 Data Retention and Deletion
Customer data is retained for the duration of the contract. Upon contract termination, customer
data is deleted within 90 days per the Customer Deletion Workflow. Customers may request
an earlier deletion or a data export before deletion. Data export is provided in JSON or CSV
format within 10 business days of request.
6. Restrictions
6.1 Fair Use
The uptime guarantee and support commitments assume fair use of the platform. The following
are not covered:
• Automated load testing against production endpoints without prior coordination with
Acme Corp.
• Intentional misuse or abuse of the API (e.g., denial-of-service patterns, credential stuffing).
• Use of the platform for purposes not described in the customer’s Order Form.
4


6.2 Restrictions Applicable to Enterprise Users
Enterprise users are subject to the following restrictions:
• Data export is limited to 50 GB per request via the bulk export API. Larger exports require
TAM coordination.
• Custom SSO configurations must use SAML 2.0 or OIDC. Legacy protocols (LDAP direct
bind) are not supported.
• Sandbox environments are limited to 3 per customer and are not covered by the uptime
SLA.
• Beta features are opted into explicitly and are excluded from SLA coverage until general
availability.
• Sub-processing (sharing customer data with Acme Corp sub-processors) is governed by
the Data Processing Agreement and requires customer consent per the terms of that
agreement.
7. Term and Termination
7.1 Term
This SLA is effective for the duration of the customer’s Enterprise Agreement. It renews auto-
matically with each contract renewal unless either party provides written notice of changes at
least 60 days before renewal.
7.2 Termination for Cause
Either party may terminate the agreement if:
• The other party commits a material breach and fails to cure it within 30 days of written
notice.
• The uptime guarantee is not met for 3 or more months in any 12-month period (customer
termination right only).
7.3 Effect of Termination
Upon termination, Acme Corp will provide a data export and delete customer data per Section
5.3. Prepaid fees for the unused portion of the term are refunded on a prorated basis if Acme
Corp terminates for convenience or the customer terminates for cause.
8. Amendments
This SLA may be amended by mutual written agreement. Acme Corp may update non-material
terms (e.g., support channel URLs, contact details) with 30 days’ notice. Material changes (up-
time commitment, credit schedule, support response times) require written customer consent.

# Expense Report Approval Policy

**Effective Date:** November 1, 2020
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp employees

## 1. Approval Thresholds

Expense reports require the following approvals based on the total amount:

| Expense Amount | Required Approval |
|---|---|
| Under $500 | Direct manager |
| $500 – $10,000 | Direct manager and department head |
| Over $10,000 | Direct manager, department head, and CFO |

All approvals must be completed in the expense management system before reimbursement is processed. Verbal approvals are not accepted.

## 2. Eligible Expenses

Reimbursable business expenses include:

- Business travel (airfare, lodging, ground transportation, meals).
- Client entertainment and meals (with business purpose documented).
- Conference and training registration fees.
- Professional development materials (books, subscriptions, courses).
- Office supplies not available through the standard procurement process.
- Home office equipment covered under the Remote Work Policy stipend.

## 3. Non-Reimbursable Expenses

The following are not eligible for reimbursement:

- Personal expenses of any kind.
- Alcohol (except at approved client entertainment events, limited to 2 drinks per person).
- First-class or business-class airfare (economy or premium economy only, unless the flight exceeds 6 hours).
- Hotel room upgrades beyond the company's negotiated rate.
- Spouse or family travel costs.
- Traffic violations or parking tickets.

## 4. Submission Requirements

- Expense reports must be submitted within 30 days of the expense being incurred.
- Receipts are required for all individual expenses over $25.
- Each line item must include a business purpose description.
- International expenses must be converted to USD using the exchange rate on the date of the transaction.

Reports submitted more than 60 days after the expense was incurred require VP-level approval and may be denied.

## 5. Reimbursement Timeline

Approved expense reports are reimbursed via direct deposit within 10 business days of final approval. If an approver is out of office, the employee may request delegation to an alternate approver through the HR portal.

## 6. Corporate Credit Cards

Employees at L5 and above are eligible for a corporate credit card. Corporate card expenses follow the same approval thresholds and submission requirements as out-of-pocket expenses. Unreconciled corporate card transactions older than 45 days are flagged by Finance and escalated to the employee's manager.

## 7. Policy Governance

This policy is administered by the Finance department. Questions should be directed to finance@acmecorp.com.

text:
FMLA Leave Procedure
FMLA Leave Procedure
Effective Date: September 15, 2023 Applies to: All Acme Corp employees meeting the eligibility requirements below
1. Overview
The Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) provides eligible employees with up to 12 weeks of unpaid, job-protected leave per 12-month period for qualifying reasons. This document describes the step-by-step procedure for requesting, approving, and managing FMLA leave at Acme Corp.
2. Eligibility
To qualify for FMLA leave, an employee must have completed 12 months of continuous employment at Acme Corp at the time the leave is requested. There is no minimum hours-worked threshold beyond Acme Corp’s standard full-time classification.
Employees who are unsure of their eligibility should contact Human Resources before submitting a formal request.
3. Qualifying Reasons
FMLA leave may be taken for any of the following reasons:
A serious health condition that prevents the employee from performing their job functions.
To care for a spouse, child, or parent with a serious health condition.
The birth of a child and bonding within 12 months of birth.
Placement of a child for adoption or foster care and bonding within 12 months of placement.
A qualifying exigency arising from a spouse’s, child’s, or parent’s active duty military service.
4. The FMLA Request Process
The request process consists of four steps:
Step 1 — Notify HR
The employee must notify Human Resources at least 30 days in advance of foreseeable leave. For unforeseeable leave (e.g., sudden illness, accident, emergency placement), the employee must notify HR as soon as practicable, generally within 1–2 business days of learning of the need for leave.
Notification may be made by email to hr@acmecorp.com, through the HR portal, or in person. The notification should include:
The reason for the leave (general category; detailed medical information is not required at this stage).
The expected start date.
The expected duration or, if unknown, a best estimate.
Whether the leave will be continuous or intermittent.
Step 2 — Submit Medical Certification
Within 15 calendar days of the initial notification, the employee must submit a completed medical certification form. Acme Corp uses the Department of Labor’s WH-380-E (for the employee’s own condition) or WH-380-F (for a family member’s condition).
The certification must be completed by the treating healthcare provider and must include:
The date the serious health condition began.
The probable duration of the condition.
Relevant medical facts sufficient to establish that a serious health condition exists.
For the employee’s own condition: a statement that the employee is unable to perform their job functions.
For a family member’s condition: a statement that the employee’s presence is necessary or would be beneficial.
If the certification is incomplete or unclear, HR will provide the employee with a written notice specifying what additional information is needed. The employee has 7 calendar days to provide the supplemental information.
Acme Corp reserves the right to request a second medical opinion at the company’s expense. If the first and second opinions conflict, a third opinion from a mutually agreed-upon provider is final and binding.
Step 3 — HR Reviews
Human Resources reviews the request and medical certification and responds within 5 business days with one of the following:
Approved: The leave is designated as FMLA-qualifying. HR provides the employee with a written designation notice specifying the leave dates, whether the leave is paid or unpaid, and any requirements for fitness-for-duty certification upon return.
Denied: The leave does not meet FMLA requirements. HR provides a written explanation of the reason for denial, which may include insufficient tenure, incomplete certification, or a condition that does not qualify as a serious health condition.
Additional information requested: The certification is incomplete. HR specifies what is needed and the deadline for submission.
Approved FMLA leave is tracked in the HR system. The 12-week entitlement is measured on a rolling 12-month basis, looking back from the date leave is used.
Step 4 — Manager Adjusts Workload
Once FMLA leave is approved, the employee’s manager is responsible for:
Reassigning the employee’s active tasks and projects to other team members.
Updating project timelines and deliverables to reflect the employee’s absence.
Communicating coverage plans to affected stakeholders (without disclosing the reason for the leave).
Identifying a point of contact for urgent matters that were previously handled by the employee.
Managers should complete workload adjustments within 3 business days of receiving the approval notification from HR. Human Resources is available to assist with transition planning for extended leave periods.
5. Intermittent and Reduced-Schedule Leave
FMLA leave may be taken intermittently (in separate blocks of time) or on a reduced schedule (fewer hours per day or per week) when medically necessary.
Employees requesting intermittent leave must provide a medical certification that establishes the medical necessity for the intermittent schedule, including the expected frequency and duration of episodes.
Employees on intermittent leave must follow the company’s standard call-in procedures for each absence. Intermittent leave is tracked in increments of one hour.
Acme Corp may temporarily transfer an employee on intermittent leave to an equivalent position that better accommodates the recurring schedule, with the same pay and benefits.
6. Pay and Benefits During FMLA Leave
FMLA leave is unpaid. However, employees may choose — and Acme Corp may require — the use of accrued paid vacation or sick leave concurrently with FMLA leave to receive pay during the absence.
If the employee qualifies for Short-Term Disability benefits, those benefits run concurrently with FMLA leave. The Short-Term Disability Policy provides 60% of base salary for up to 12 weeks following a 7-day waiting period.
If the employee qualifies for parental leave under the Parental Leave Policy, parental leave pay (100% of base salary) runs concurrently with FMLA leave.
During FMLA leave, the following benefits continue:
Health, dental, and vision insurance. The employee’s share of premiums must continue to be paid. If leave is unpaid, HR will arrange a payment schedule before leave begins.
Life insurance.
401(k) participation is suspended during unpaid leave; employer matching resumes upon return. Vesting credit continues to accrue.
Vacation and sick leave do not accrue during unpaid FMLA leave.
7. Return to Work
Employees returning from FMLA leave are entitled to the same or an equivalent position with the same pay, benefits, and terms of employment.
For leave taken due to the employee’s own serious health condition, a fitness-for-duty certification from the treating provider is required before the employee may return to work. The certification must confirm that the employee is able to perform the essential functions of their position. HR will notify the employee of this requirement in the designation notice.
Employees must confirm their intended return date with HR at least 5 business days before returning. If the employee does not return on the agreed-upon date and does not request an extension, Acme Corp may treat the absence as a voluntary resignation.
8. How FMLA Differs from Short-Term Disability
Employees sometimes confuse FMLA leave with Short-Term Disability (STD). These are distinct programs:
In many cases, FMLA and STD run concurrently. An employee with a serious health condition that prevents them from working may receive STD pay while FMLA provides the job protection. See the Short-Term Disability Policy for claims procedures.
9. Prohibited Actions
Acme Corp prohibits:
Interfering with an employee’s exercise of FMLA rights.
Retaliating against an employee for requesting or taking FMLA leave.
Using FMLA leave as a negative factor in performance evaluations, promotion decisions, or any other employment action.
Violations should be reported to Human Resources or the anonymous ethics hotline at 1-800-555-0142.
10. Policy Governance
This procedure is administered by Human Resources. Questions should be directed to hr@acmecorp.com or the employee’s HR Business Partner.

Harassment Policy and Reporting Procedures
Harassment Policy and Reporting Procedures
Effective Date: February 1, 2024 Applies to: All Acme Corp employees, contractors, interns,
and temporary workers
1. Policy Statement
Acme Corp maintains a zero-tolerance policy for harassment, discrimination, and retaliation
in the workplace. This policy applies to all work-related settings, including offices, company
events, business travel, and electronic communications (email, chat, video).
2. Prohibited Conduct
Harassment includes any unwelcome conduct based on race, color, religion, sex, national ori-
gin, age, disability, veteran status, sexual orientation, gender identity, or any other protected
characteristic. Prohibited conduct includes but is not limited to:
• Verbal: Slurs, epithets, derogatory comments, jokes, threats, or intimidation.
• Physical: Unwanted touching, blocking movement, physical intimidation.
• Visual: Offensive images, cartoons, or gestures displayed in the workplace or shared
electronically.
• Electronic: Harassing messages via email, chat, social media, or any company commu-
nication system.
• Sexual harassment: Unwelcome sexual advances, requests for sexual favors, or other
verbal or physical conduct of a sexual nature. This includes quid pro quo harassment
(conditioning employment benefits on sexual favors) and hostile work environment ha-
rassment (conduct that is severe or pervasive enough to create an intimidating or offen-
sive work environment).
3. Reporting
Employees who experience or witness harassment should report it promptly through any of
the following channels:
1. Direct manager or any member of management.
2. Human Resources — in person, by email to hr@acmecorp.com, or through the HR portal.
3. Anonymous ethics hotline — 1-800-555-0142, available 24/7. Reports may be made
anonymously. The hotline is operated by an independent third party.
There is no requirement to report to the direct manager first. Employees may use whichever
channel they are most comfortable with. Reports made through any channel receive the same
level of investigation and follow-up.
1


Managers who receive a report of harassment are required to notify Human Resources within
24 hours, regardless of whether the reporting employee asks them to. Failure to escalate a
report is a policy violation.
4. Investigation Process
All reports are investigated promptly by Human Resources or, for reports involving senior lead-
ership, by an external investigator.
4.1 Timeline
• Acknowledgment: The reporting employee receives acknowledgment within 24 hours
of the report.
• Investigation initiation: A formal investigation begins within 48 hours of the report
being received by Human Resources.
• Interviews: The investigator interviews the reporting employee, the respondent, and
any identified witnesses. All parties are reminded of the no-retaliation policy.
• Completion target: Investigations are completed within 30 calendar days. If additional
time is needed, Human Resources notifies all parties of the delay and revised timeline.
• Outcome notification: Both the reporting employee and the respondent are notified of
the investigation outcome in writing.
4.2 Confidentiality
Investigations are conducted with as much confidentiality as possible, consistent with a thor-
ough investigation. Information is shared only with those who have a legitimate need to know.
All participants are required to maintain confidentiality about the investigation.
4.3 Interim Measures
During an investigation, Human Resources may implement interim measures to protect the
reporting employee, including:
• Temporary reassignment of the reporting employee or the respondent.
• Modification of reporting structure.
• Adjustment of work schedules or locations.
• No-contact directives.
Interim measures are not disciplinary and do not presume a finding of responsibility.
5. Disciplinary Action
If the investigation substantiates a violation of this policy, disciplinary action is taken based on
the severity of the conduct. Possible actions include:
• Written warning.
• Mandatory additional training.
• Demotion or reassignment.
• Suspension without pay.
• Termination of employment.
For severe or repeated violations, termination is the standard outcome. The Chief Compliance
Officer and VP of the respondent’s department approve all disciplinary actions resulting from
harassment investigations.
2


6. No-Retaliation Clause
Retaliation against any employee who reports a concern in good faith, participates in an inves-
tigation, or opposes conduct they reasonably believe violates this policy is strictly prohibited.
Retaliation includes termination, demotion, reduction in hours, reassignment to undesirable
duties, or any other adverse employment action.
Employees who believe they have experienced retaliation should report it immediately through
any of the channels listed in Section 3. Retaliation claims are investigated using the same
process described in Section 4.
Any employee found to have retaliated against a reporter is subject to disciplinary action up
to and including termination, regardless of whether the original harassment complaint is sub-
stantiated.
7. Mandatory Training
All employees are required to complete harassment prevention training annually. Training cov-
ers:
• Recognition of harassment, discrimination, and retaliation.
• Reporting procedures and available channels.
• Bystander intervention techniques.
• Manager-specific responsibilities (for people managers).
New hires must complete training within their first 30 days of employment. Training completion
is tracked in the HR system. Failure to complete training by the annual deadline may result in
suspension of the employee’s system access until training is completed.
8. Policy Governance
This policy is administered by Human Resources with oversight from the Chief Compliance
Officer. The policy is reviewed annually. Questions should be directed to hr@acmecorp.com.
3

# Hardware Request Procedure

**Effective Date:** September 15, 2024
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp employees

## 1. Eligible Hardware

Employees may request the following through this procedure:

- Laptops (new or replacement).
- External monitors (up to two per employee).
- Docking stations and USB-C hubs.
- Keyboards, mice, and other peripherals.
- Hardware upgrades (RAM, SSD) for existing company-issued devices, where supported by the model.
- Ergonomic equipment (standing desk converters, monitor arms) — coordinate with Facilities for desk-mounted items.

Mobile devices (phones, tablets) are requested through a separate process managed by the IT mobile team.

## 2. Request Process

1. **Submit a request** through the IT self-service portal at it-portal.acmecorp.com. Select "Hardware Request" and specify the item, business justification, and any urgency.
2. **Manager approval.** All hardware requests require direct manager approval. The manager receives an email notification and must approve or deny within 3 business days. Requests not approved within 5 business days are automatically closed.
3. **IT review.** After manager approval, IT Operations reviews the request for compatibility, inventory availability, and policy compliance. IT may suggest an alternative if the requested item is out of stock or not on the approved hardware list.
4. **Procurement.** If the item is in inventory, IT provisions and ships it. If the item must be ordered, IT places the order and provides an estimated delivery date.
5. **Delivery and setup.** Hardware is delivered to the employee's office location or shipped to their home address (for approved remote employees). IT pre-configures laptops with the standard image, disk encryption, and endpoint agent before delivery.

## 3. Delivery SLAs

| Item | In-Stock SLA | Out-of-Stock SLA |
|---|---|---|
| Laptop (standard model) | 3 business days | 10–15 business days |
| Monitor | 3 business days | 7–10 business days |
| Peripherals (keyboard, mouse, dock) | 2 business days | 5–7 business days |
| Hardware upgrade (RAM, SSD) | 5 business days (includes scheduling) | 10–15 business days |

SLAs are measured from the date of IT approval (not the date of the employee's initial request).

## 4. Budget

Standard hardware (laptops, monitors, peripherals) is funded by the IT budget and does not require department budget approval. Non-standard or premium hardware (e.g., high-end GPU workstations, ultrawide monitors) requires department budget approval in addition to manager approval.

The IT self-service portal indicates which items are standard and which require additional approval.

## 5. Returns and Replacements

- Defective hardware is replaced at no cost. Submit a support ticket to IT with the asset tag and a description of the issue.
- Hardware reaching end-of-life (typically 4 years for laptops, 5 years for monitors) is proactively replaced by IT. Employees are notified 30 days before the scheduled replacement.
- All returned hardware must be surrendered to IT for secure data wiping and asset tracking.

## 6. Policy Governance

This procedure is maintained by IT Operations. Questions should be directed to it-support@acmecorp.com or Slack #it-help.

---

Health Plans Comparison
Health Plans Comparison — 2025 Plan Year
Effective: January 1, 2025 Enrollment deadline: November 15, 2024 Applies to: All full-
time Acme Corp employees and their eligible dependents
Plan Overview
Acme Corp offers three health insurance plans.
All plans use the Anthem Blue Cross PPO
network. The plan you choose determines your deductible, coinsurance split, out-of-pocket
maximum, and whether dental and vision coverage is included.

Basic Plan
The Basic plan has the lowest monthly premium and is designed for employees who are gen-
erally healthy and do not anticipate frequent medical visits.
Key features:
• Annual deductible of $200 per individual ($400 per family). The deductible must be met
before the plan begins paying its share of covered services, except for preventive care.
• After the deductible, the plan pays 80% of covered services and the employee pays 20%
until the out-of-pocket maximum is reached.
• Out-of-pocket maximum of $6,000 per individual ($12,000 per family). Once reached, the
plan pays 100% of covered services for the remainder of the plan year.
• Preventive care (annual physicals, immunizations, screenings) is covered at 100% with
no deductible.
• Prescription drug coverage uses a three-tier formulary: $10 generic, $35 preferred brand,
$60 non-preferred brand.
Not included in the Basic plan:
1


• Dental coverage. Employees on the Basic plan who want dental must enroll in the stan-
dalone dental plan ($28/month individual, $72/month family).
• Vision coverage. Employees on the Basic plan who want vision must enroll in the stan-
dalone vision plan ($12/month individual, $30/month family).
• Fertility treatment coverage.
• Chiropractic and acupuncture services.
• International travel medical coverage.
Standard Plan
The Standard plan provides a middle-ground option with moderate premiums and a broader
benefit set than Basic.
Key features:
• Annual deductible of $500 per individual ($1,000 per family).
• After the deductible, the plan pays 70% and the employee pays 30%.
• Out-of-pocket maximum of $5,000 per individual ($10,000 per family).
• Preventive care covered at 100% with no deductible.
• Prescription drug coverage: $10 generic, $30 preferred brand, $50 non-preferred brand.
• Telehealth visits: $15 copay (no deductible).
• Urgent care visits: $50 copay after deductible.
Not included in the Standard plan:
• Dental coverage. Same standalone option as described under Basic.
• Vision coverage. Same standalone option as described under Basic.
• International travel medical coverage.
Included in Standard but not in Basic:
• Fertility treatment coverage up to a $15,000 lifetime maximum.
• Chiropractic services: 20 visits per plan year.
• Acupuncture: 12 visits per plan year.
Premium Plan
The Premium plan offers the most comprehensive coverage with the lowest out-of-pocket costs.
It is the only plan that includes dental and vision.
Key features:
• No annual deductible. The plan begins paying its share from the first covered service.
• Coinsurance of 90/10 (plan pays 90%, employee pays 10%).
• Out-of-pocket maximum of $2,500 per individual ($5,000 per family).
• Preventive care covered at 100%.
• Prescription drug coverage: $5 generic, $20 preferred brand, $40 non-preferred brand.
• Telehealth visits: $0 copay.
• Urgent care visits: $25 copay, no deductible.
Dental coverage (included):
• Preventive (cleanings, exams, x-rays): covered at 100%, two visits per year.
• Basic restorative (fillings, extractions): covered at 80% after a $50 dental deductible.
• Major restorative (crowns, bridges, root canals): covered at 50% after dental deductible.
• Orthodontia (dependents under 19): covered at 50% up to a $2,000 lifetime maximum.
• Annual dental maximum benefit: $2,000 per covered individual.
Vision coverage (included):
2


• Annual eye exam: covered at 100%.
• Eyeglass frames: $200 allowance every 24 months.
• Lenses: covered at 100% (standard single, bifocal, or progressive).
• Contact lenses: $150 annual allowance in lieu of frames.
Also included in Premium but not in Basic or Standard:
• Fertility treatment coverage up to a $25,000 lifetime maximum.
• Chiropractic services: unlimited visits per plan year.
• Acupuncture: 24 visits per plan year.
• International travel medical coverage: emergency services while traveling outside the
U.S., up to $100,000 per trip.
• Mental health parity: outpatient therapy visits at $15 copay with no annual limit.
Choosing a Plan
Consider the following when selecting a plan:
• Basic works best for individuals who rarely visit the doctor, want the lowest premium, and
are comfortable paying more when they do need care. Pair with an HSA to save pre-tax
dollars for medical expenses.
• Standard suits employees who anticipate moderate medical use — a few specialist vis-
its, a planned procedure, or ongoing prescriptions — and want fertility and chiropractic
coverage without the Premium price.
• Premium is designed for employees with families, ongoing medical needs, or anyone
who values predictable low out-of-pocket costs. The included dental and vision makes it
the simplest option for comprehensive coverage.
Enrollment
Plan elections are made during the annual open enrollment period, which closes November 15.
Elections take effect January 1 of the following plan year.
Mid-year changes are permitted only within 30 days of a qualifying life event: marriage, divorce,
birth or adoption, loss of other coverage, or change in dependent eligibility.
New hires may elect a plan within 30 days of their start date. Coverage begins on the first day
of employment.
Enrollment is managed through the benefits portal at benefits.acmecorp.com. For assistance,
contact the Benefits team at benefits@acmecorp.com or ext. 4200.

# HIPAA Compliance Overview

**Effective Date:** October 1, 2024
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp systems, teams, and personnel that process protected health information (PHI) on behalf of healthcare customers

## 1. Background

Acme Corp processes electronic protected health information (ePHI) for a subset of its customer base under executed Business Associate Agreements (BAAs). As a business associate under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), Acme Corp is required to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of ePHI.

This document summarizes Acme Corp's HIPAA obligations and the internal controls that satisfy them. It is intended as a reference for employees who work with healthcare customer data. For the full regulatory text, refer to 45 CFR Parts 160 and 164.

## 2. The HIPAA Privacy Rule

The HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes standards for the protection of individually identifiable health information. Under the Privacy Rule, Acme Corp must:

- Use and disclose protected health information only as permitted or required by the Privacy Rule or as authorized by the individual.
- Apply the **minimum necessary standard**: when using, disclosing, or requesting PHI, limit the information to the minimum amount necessary to accomplish the intended purpose.
- Provide individuals with access to their PHI upon request.
- Maintain records of disclosures for at least 6 years.

The minimum necessary standard does not apply to disclosures for treatment purposes or to disclosures required by law. In all other cases, employees must limit PHI access and sharing to what is strictly needed for the task at hand.

## 3. The HIPAA Security Rule

The HIPAA Security Rule requires covered entities and business associates to implement safeguards to protect ePHI. Acme Corp's controls are organized into three categories:

### 3.1 Administrative Safeguards

- Designated Security Officer responsible for HIPAA security compliance (function held by the Data Protection Officer).
- Workforce training on HIPAA requirements (annual, per the Mandatory Training Catalog).
- Risk analysis conducted annually to identify threats to ePHI.
- Sanctions for workforce members who violate HIPAA policies.
- Business Associate Agreements with all subcontractors who access ePHI.

### 3.2 Physical Safeguards

- Facility access controls (badge readers, visitor escort requirements).
- Workstation security (screen lock, full disk encryption, endpoint agent).
- Device disposal procedures (cryptographic erasure before decommissioning).

### 3.3 Technical Safeguards

The HIPAA Security Rule, Section 164.312(a)(1), requires access controls that restrict access to ePHI to authorized persons and software. Acme Corp implements the following access controls for ePHI systems:

- **Unique user identification:** Every user has a unique account. Shared accounts are prohibited for ePHI systems.
- **Emergency access procedure:** A documented break-glass process allows emergency access to ePHI systems when normal access mechanisms are unavailable.
- **Automatic logoff:** Sessions on ePHI systems time out after 15 minutes of inactivity.
- **Encryption and decryption:** ePHI is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3) per the Encryption Standards.

Additional technical controls include:

- **Audit controls (Section 164.312(b)):** All access to ePHI is logged with user identity, timestamp, action performed, and records accessed. Logs are retained per the Audit Log Retention policy (1 year hot, 6 years cold).
- **Integrity controls (Section 164.312(c)(1)):** Mechanisms to authenticate ePHI and detect unauthorized alteration, including database checksums and application-level integrity verification.
- **Transmission security (Section 164.312(e)(1)):** All ePHI transmitted over electronic networks is encrypted using TLS 1.3. No exceptions for internal service-to-service traffic.

## 4. Breach Notification

Under the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, Acme Corp must notify affected individuals, the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and in some cases the media, following a breach of unsecured PHI.

- **Individual notification:** Within 60 days of discovering the breach.
- **HHS notification:** Within 60 days for breaches affecting 500 or more individuals. Breaches affecting fewer than 500 individuals are reported to HHS annually.
- **State attorneys general:** Notification as required by state breach notification laws.

Internally, the breach response process is coordinated per the Incident Response Runbook. The Data Protection Officer and Chief Compliance Officer are notified within 4 hours of breach confirmation.

## 5. Rules for Keeping Medical Records Private

Employees who handle protected health information must follow these rules to keep medical records private:

- Access ePHI only when required for your assigned duties. Do not browse patient or customer health records out of curiosity.
- Do not discuss PHI in public areas, shared office spaces, or non-secure communication channels.
- Do not copy ePHI to personal devices, personal email, or personal cloud storage.
- Verify the identity and authorization of anyone requesting PHI before disclosing it.
- Report any suspected unauthorized access or disclosure to security@acmecorp.com immediately.

Violations of these rules are subject to disciplinary action and may result in civil or criminal penalties under HIPAA.

## 6. Scope of Acme Corp's HIPAA Obligations

Not all Acme Corp systems or customers are subject to HIPAA. HIPAA requirements apply only to:

- Systems designated as "HIPAA in scope" in the service catalog.
- Customer accounts with an executed BAA.
- Employees and contractors who access ePHI as part of their role.

Employees who do not work with healthcare customers or ePHI are not subject to the HIPAA-specific controls described in this document, though the general security policies (IT Security Policy, Access Control Policy, Data Classification Policy) apply to all employees.

## 7. Policy Governance

This document is maintained by the Information Security team in coordination with the Data Protection Officer and the Chief Compliance Officer (Jordan Patel). It is reviewed annually and updated to reflect changes in HIPAA regulations or Acme Corp's BAA portfolio. Questions should be directed to security@acmecorp.com.

---

# HR Data Retention Schedule

**Effective Date:** June 1, 2024
**Approved by:** General Counsel, Data Protection Officer (Mariana Costa)
**Applies to:** All HR records maintained by Acme Corp, regardless of storage format or location

---

## 1. Purpose

This schedule defines the retention periods for HR-related data. It supplements the company-wide Data Retention Policy with HR-specific requirements driven by employment law, tax regulations, and operational needs. Where this schedule specifies a longer or shorter retention period than the general policy, this schedule controls for HR records.

## 2. Retention Periods

| Record Type | Retention Period | Regulatory Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Job applications (hired candidates) | 2 years from hire date | EEOC, OFCCP |
| Job applications (not hired) | 2 years from application date | EEOC, OFCCP |
| Employee personnel files | Duration of employment + 7 years | Federal and state employment law |
| Payroll and tax records | 7 years from date of record | IRS (§6001), SOX |
| I-9 employment verification | 3 years from hire date or 1 year after termination, whichever is later | USCIS |
| Benefits enrollment records | Duration of enrollment + 7 years | ERISA |
| Training completion records | 5 years from date of completion | SOC 2 evidence, OSHA (for safety training) |
| Performance reviews | 7 years from review date | Internal policy, litigation hold readiness |
| Disciplinary records | Duration of employment + 7 years | Federal and state employment law |
| Workers' compensation claims | 7 years from claim closure | State law (varies) |
| FMLA records | 3 years from leave end date | DOL (29 CFR §825.500) |
| Exit interviews | 3 years from separation date | Internal policy |
| Background check results | 2 years from hire date | FCRA |
| EEO-1 reports | 3 years | EEOC |
| OSHA injury/illness logs | 5 years from end of calendar year | OSHA (29 CFR §1904.33) |

## 3. EU Employee Records

For employees based in Acme Corp's EU offices or EU-based remote employees, additional constraints apply per GDPR and local labor law:

- Personal identification data follows the Data Retention Policy's EU-Specific Requirements: deleted or anonymized within 3 years of the end of the employment relationship unless a specific legal basis for longer retention exists.
- Payroll and tax records may be retained for the full 7 years where required by local tax law (e.g., German tax law requires 10 years for certain financial records — the longer local requirement applies).
- Performance reviews for EU employees are retained for 3 years after separation, not 7, unless a legal hold is in effect.

The Data Protection Officer reviews EU HR retention schedules annually to ensure alignment with local requirements in each EU jurisdiction where Acme Corp has employees.

## 4. Data Minimization

HR must periodically review retained records to ensure that only the minimum necessary data is kept:

- Sensitive personal data (medical information, disability documentation, background check details) that is no longer needed for its original purpose should be deleted even if the broader employee file is still within its retention period.
- Interview notes and scoring rubrics for candidates not hired should be limited to the standardized evaluation form. Informal notes should be deleted after the hiring decision is finalized.

## 5. Deletion Process

When a record reaches the end of its retention period:

1. The HR system generates a deletion queue during the first week of each quarter.
2. The HR Records Coordinator reviews the queue and confirms that no legal hold or active investigation applies.
3. Electronic records are deleted from the HRIS and any backup systems per the Data Retention Policy's Data Deletion Procedures.
4. Physical records (if any) are shredded using a cross-cut shredder or a certified document destruction vendor.
5. The Data Protection Officer certifies the deletion.

## 6. Legal Holds

If a legal hold is issued by the General Counsel, all HR records within the scope of the hold are preserved regardless of the retention schedule. Legal holds override this schedule until released. See the Data Retention Policy for the legal hold process.

## 7. Policy Governance

This schedule is maintained by Human Resources in coordination with Legal and the Data Protection Officer (Mariana Costa). It is reviewed annually. Questions should be directed to hr@acmecorp.com.

---

*Version 1.1 — Last revised June 1, 2024*
title: HR Data Retention Schedule

# HSA/FSA Policy

**Effective Date:** October 15, 2024
**Applies to:** All benefits-eligible Acme Corp employees

## 1. Health Savings Account (HSA)

### Eligibility

Employees enrolled in the Basic or Standard health plan are eligible to open an HSA. Employees enrolled in the Premium plan are not eligible, as that plan does not meet the IRS high-deductible health plan (HDHP) requirement.

### Contribution Limits (2025)

| Coverage Level | Employee + Employer Annual Limit |
|---|---|
| Individual | $4,300 |
| Family | $8,550 |
| Catch-up (age 55+) | Additional $1,000 |

Acme Corp contributes $500 (individual) or $1,000 (family) annually to each eligible employee's HSA in quarterly installments. This amount counts toward the IRS annual limit.

### Key Features

- Contributions are pre-tax (or tax-deductible if contributed outside payroll).
- Earnings grow tax-free.
- Withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free.
- Funds roll over indefinitely — there is no "use it or lose it" deadline.
- The account is owned by the employee and remains theirs if they leave Acme Corp.

### Qualified Expenses

HSA funds may be used for medical, dental, and vision expenses not covered by insurance, including deductibles, copays, coinsurance, prescription drugs, and certain over-the-counter items. A full list of qualified expenses is available on the benefits portal.

## 2. Flexible Spending Account (FSA)

### Eligibility

All benefits-eligible employees may open an FSA regardless of which health plan they are enrolled in. Acme Corp offers two FSA types:

- **Healthcare FSA:** For medical, dental, and vision expenses not covered by insurance. Employees enrolled in an HSA-eligible plan may only open a Limited-Purpose FSA (dental and vision expenses only).
- **Dependent Care FSA:** For eligible dependent care expenses such as daycare, preschool, and before/after-school programs.

### Contribution Limits (2025)

| FSA Type | Annual Maximum |
|---|---|
| Healthcare FSA | $3,300 |
| Limited-Purpose FSA | $3,300 |
| Dependent Care FSA | $5,000 ($2,500 if married filing separately) |

Acme Corp does not contribute to FSA accounts.

### Rollover Rules

- **Healthcare and Limited-Purpose FSA:** Up to $640 of unused funds may roll over into the next plan year. Balances above $640 remaining after the March 31 run-out period are forfeited.
- **Dependent Care FSA:** No rollover. Unused funds remaining after the March 31 run-out period are forfeited.

### Run-Out Period

Employees have until March 31 of the following plan year to submit claims for expenses incurred during the prior plan year. Claims submitted after March 31 are not eligible for reimbursement.

## 3. Enrollment

HSA and FSA elections are made during the annual open enrollment period (closes November 15) or within 30 days of a qualifying life event. HSA contribution changes may also be made at any time during the plan year.

Enrollment and contribution management are handled through the benefits portal at benefits.acmecorp.com.

## 4. Policy Governance

This policy is administered by Human Resources. The HSA custodian is Fidelity Investments. The FSA administrator is WageWorks. Questions should be directed to benefits@acmecorp.com or ext. 4200.
title: HSA/FSA Policy

# Incident Response Runbook

**Document ID:** ITSEC-010
**Effective Date:** September 1, 2024
**Applies to:** All Acme Corp engineering and operations personnel

## 1. Severity Classification

| Severity | Label | Definition | Response SLA |
|---|---|---|---|
| SEV1 | Critical | Active data breach, ransomware, complete production outage affecting all customers, security compromise with data exposure | 15 minutes to assemble response team |
| SEV2 | High | Major feature unavailable or severely degraded, confirmed unauthorized access to Restricted data, exploitation of a known vulnerability | 1 hour |
| SEV3 | Medium | Suspicious activity under investigation, successful phishing attempt, minor service degradation with workaround available, policy violation | 4 hours |
| SEV4 | Low | Failed attack attempt, minor policy deviation, security question, single-user access issue | Next business day |

## 2. On-Call Structure

### 2.1 Rotation

The engineering on-call rotation follows a weekly schedule (Monday 9 AM to Monday 9 AM). Two engineers are on call at all times:

- **Primary:** First responder for all alerts. Acknowledges within the SLA response time.
- **Secondary:** Backup if the primary does not acknowledge within 5 minutes. Also available for parallel investigation during SEV1/SEV2.

The on-call schedule is managed in PagerDuty. Swaps must be arranged at least 24 hours in advance and logged in PagerDuty.

### 2.2 On-Call Handoff

At each rotation change, the outgoing on-call engineer conducts a 15-minute handoff meeting with the incoming engineer covering:

- Active incidents and their current status.
- Ongoing investigations or monitoring watch items.
- Any known fragile systems or upcoming deployments that increase risk.
- Unresolved alerts that were deferred during the rotation.

Handoff notes are posted to the #oncall-handoff Slack channel.

## 3. Incident Response Process

### Step 1 — Detection and Reporting

Incidents may be detected through:

- Automated monitoring alerts (PagerDuty, Datadog, Grafana).
- Employee reports to security@acmecorp.com or the #security-incidents Slack channel.
- Customer reports through the support team.
- External notification (e.g., vulnerability researcher, partner, law enforcement).

Any employee who suspects a security incident must report it immediately. Do not attempt to investigate or remediate independently.

### Step 2 — Acknowledgment and Triage

The on-call engineer acknowledges the alert within the SLA response time for the severity level. Acknowledgment is recorded in PagerDuty and auto-posted to #incidents in Slack.

During triage, the on-call engineer:

1. Confirms the severity classification.
2. Assesses blast radius: how many customers, systems, or data sets are affected?
3. Determines if the issue is escalating, stable, or resolving.
4. For SEV1/SEV2: pages the incident commander (InfoSec team lead) and opens a dedicated incident Slack channel.

### Step 3 — Escalation Process for Critical Incidents

For SEV1 and SEV2 incidents, the following escalation chain applies:

| Time from Detection | Action | Responsible |
|---|---|---|
| 0–15 minutes | Primary on-call acknowledges, begins triage | On-call engineer |
| 15 minutes | Incident commander paged if not already engaged | On-call engineer |
| 15–30 minutes | Incident commander assembles response team, opens war room | Incident commander |
| 30 minutes | VP of Engineering notified | Incident commander |
| 1 hour | Status page updated with initial customer-facing communication | Incident commander + Customer Success |
| 2 hours | Chief Compliance Officer notified if data breach is suspected | Incident commander |
| 4 hours | Executive leadership briefed if incident is not contained | VP of Engineering |

For SEV3/SEV4 incidents, the on-call engineer handles triage and resolution independently, escalating to the InfoSec team lead only if the situation warrants reclassification to SEV2 or above.

### Step 4 — Containment

The response team takes immediate actions to limit scope and impact:

- Isolate affected systems from the network.
- Revoke compromised credentials and rotate affected secrets.
- Block malicious IPs or domains at the firewall and WAF.
- Roll back the most recent deployment if the incident correlates with a release.
- Enable circuit breakers for affected downstream services.
- Preserve forensic evidence (do not reboot or rebuild affected systems until forensic snapshots are taken).

Containment actions are logged in the incident channel in real time.

### Step 5 — Investigation

The incident commander coordinates the investigation:

- Root cause analysis: what vulnerability or failure led to the incident?
- Scope determination: what data, systems, or customers were affected?
- Forensic evidence collection: logs, disk images, memory dumps as appropriate.
- Timeline reconstruction: what happened, in what order, starting from when?

Investigation findings are documented in the incident record. External forensic investigators may be engaged for SEV1 incidents at the discretion of the VP of Engineering and Legal.

### Step 6 — Remediation

Once the root cause is identified:

- Apply the fix (patch, configuration change, code fix).
- Verify the fix through testing in staging before production deployment.
- Restore affected systems from clean backups if necessary.
- Re-enable any services or features that were disabled during containment.
- Confirm that monitoring shows normal behavior.

### Step 7 — Communication

**Internal communication:**

- Incident status updates posted to #incidents every 30 minutes during SEV1/SEV2.
- Post-resolution summary sent to engineering-all@acmecorp.com within 4 hours of resolution.

**Customer communication:**

- Status page updated within 15 minutes of SEV1/SEV2 acknowledgment, and updated at least every hour until resolution.
- Direct notification to affected customers within 24 hours of resolution for incidents involving data access or service degradation exceeding 1 hour.

**Regulatory communication:**

See Section 4 (Breach Notification).

### Step 8 — Postmortem

A blameless postmortem is conducted within 5 business days of incident resolution. The postmortem document must include:

- **Timeline:** Chronological sequence of events from detection through resolution.
- **Root cause:** Technical explanation of what failed and why.
- **Customer impact:** Number of customers affected, duration, data exposure (if any).
- **What went well:** Response actions that were effective.
- **What went poorly:** Gaps in detection, communication, or response.
- **Action items:** At least two concrete follow-up actions, each with an owner and a due date.

Postmortems are reviewed in the weekly engineering team meeting and archived in the postmortem repository. Action items are tracked to completion in the project management system.

## 4. Breach Notification

In the event of a confirmed data breach involving personal data:

### 4.1 Internal Notification

- The Data Protection Officer is notified within 4 hours of breach confirmation.
- The Chief Compliance Officer (Jordan Patel) is notified within 4 hours.
- Legal counsel is engaged within 8 hours.

### 4.2 Regulatory Notification

- **GDPR:** The relevant supervisory authority must be notified within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, per GDPR Article 33.
- **HIPAA:** If PHI is involved, notification follows the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule. The HIPAA Privacy Officer (a function of the Data Protection Officer) coordinates the notification.
- **State laws:** Notification to affected individuals follows applicable state breach notification laws. Legal determines which state laws apply based on the residency of affected individuals.

### 4.3 Customer Notification

Affected customers are notified in writing (email and, where appropriate, in-app notification) with:

- A description of the incident and the data involved.
- The date or date range of the breach.
- Steps Acme Corp is taking to remediate.
- Steps the customer can take to protect themselves.
- Contact information for questions.

## 5. Tools and Resources

| Tool | Purpose | Access |
|---|---|---|
| PagerDuty | Alerting and on-call management | All engineering |
| Grafana | Infrastructure and application metrics | All engineering |
| Datadog | Infrastructure health and container metrics | All engineering |
| Sentry | Application error tracking | All engineering |
| Slack (#incidents, #security-incidents) | Real-time coordination | All employees |
| Status page (status.acmecorp.com) | Customer-facing incident communication | Incident commanders |
| Postmortem template | Standardized postmortem format | Confluence / wiki |

## 6. Policy Governance

This runbook is maintained by the Information Security team and reviewed quarterly. The VP of Engineering is the process owner. Questions should be directed to security@acmecorp.com.

---

*Version 3.0 — Last revised September 1, 2024*
title: Incident Response Runbook

IT Asset Management Policy
IT Asset Management Policy
Effective Date: October 1, 2022 Applies to: All Acme Corp-owned IT hardware and software assets
1. Asset Tagging
All IT hardware assets must be assigned a unique asset tag upon procurement. Tags follow the format ACME-HW-XXXXXX (six-digit sequential number). Tags are affixed as physical stickers on laptops, monitors, servers, and networking equipment, and recorded in the IT asset management system.
Software licenses are tracked under a separate identifier format: ACME-SW-XXXXXX.
2. Inventory
IT Operations maintains a centralized asset inventory that records:
3. Inventory Reconciliation
IT Operations conducts inventory reconciliation on the following schedule:
Quarterly: Automated reconciliation between the asset management system and the endpoint agent inventory. Discrepancies (devices in one system but not the other) are investigated within 10 business days.
Annually: Physical spot-check of 20% of assets selected at random. Spot-check results are documented and any discrepancies are resolved within 30 days.
Reconciliation results are reported to the VP of Engineering and retained for SOC 2 audit evidence.
4. End-of-Life and Disposal
Assets reach end-of-life based on the following schedule:
4.1 Disposal Process
IT Operations marks the asset as “decommissioned” in the inventory system.
All data is wiped using cryptographic erasure (destroying the disk encryption key) or NIST 800-88 compliant media sanitization.
The data wipe is certified in writing by the IT technician performing the wipe.
The asset is either recycled through an R2-certified electronics recycler or donated to an approved nonprofit.
The disposal record (asset tag, wipe certification, disposal method, date) is retained for 7 years.
4.2 Prohibited Disposal Methods
Assets must not be discarded in general waste or recycling bins.
Assets must not be sold or given to employees.
Storage media (hard drives, SSDs) must not be disposed of without certified data destruction.
5. Lost or Stolen Assets
Lost or stolen assets must be reported per the Laptop Security Requirements (for laptops) or to IT Operations (for other equipment). IT Operations updates the asset status and initiates a remote wipe where applicable.
6. Policy Governance
This policy is maintained by IT Operations. Questions should be directed to it-ops@acmecorp.com.

IT Security Policy
Acme Corp IT Security Policy
Document ID: ITSEC-001 Effective Date: November 1, 2024 Supersedes: Version 3.1
(March 2023) Approved by: VP of Engineering, Chief Compliance Officer Classification: Con-
fidential
1. Purpose and Scope
1.1 Purpose
This policy establishes the information security requirements for all Acme Corp systems, net-
works, and data. It defines the access controls, authentication standards, encryption require-
ments, and incident handling procedures that protect the confidentiality, integrity, and avail-
ability of company and customer information.
1.2 Scope
This policy applies to:
• All Acme Corp employees, contractors, temporary workers, and interns.
• All company-owned and company-managed information systems, including on-premises
infrastructure, cloud services, and SaaS applications.
• All data processed, stored, or transmitted by Acme Corp, regardless of format or location.
1.3 Related Policies
This policy should be read in conjunction with:
• Data Classification Policy
• Acceptable Use Policy
• Encryption Standards
• Incident Response Runbook
• Backup and Recovery Policy
• Vulnerability Management Policy
• Employee Monitoring Policy
• Patch Management Procedure
1


2. Governance and Roles
2.1 Information Security Team
The Information Security (InfoSec) team, reporting to the VP of Engineering, is responsible for:
• Developing and maintaining security policies and standards.
• Conducting risk assessments and security audits.
• Managing the vulnerability disclosure and remediation process.
• Leading incident response efforts.
• Providing security training and awareness programs.
2.2 Chief Compliance Officer
The Chief Compliance Officer (Jordan Patel) oversees the alignment of security policies with
regulatory requirements including SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA, and applicable data protection
laws.
2.3 System Owners
Each information system has a designated owner responsible for:
• Ensuring that access controls are appropriate for the data classification level of the sys-
tem.
• Reviewing and approving access requests.
• Conducting quarterly access reviews.
• Reporting security incidents involving their system.
2.4 All Employees
Every employee is responsible for:
• Complying with this policy and all related security policies.
• Completing mandatory security awareness training annually.
• Reporting suspected security incidents to the InfoSec team immediately.
• Protecting credentials and not sharing accounts.
3. Data Classification and Handling
3.1 Classification Tiers
All data processed by Acme Corp is classified into one of four tiers:
Tier
Label
Description
Examples
1
Public
Information approved
for external
distribution
Marketing materials,
published API docs,
press releases
2
Internal

information for
employee use

team wiki, meeting
notes
2


Tier

Description
Examples
3
Confidential
Sensitive business
information
Financial data,
customer lists,
strategic plans,
source code
4
Restricted
Highly sensitive;
regulatory or legal
obligations
PII, PHI, credentials,
security audit results,
encryption keys
3.2 Handling Requirements
Requirement
Internal
Confidential
Restricted
Encryption at rest

Required

Encryption in transit
Required (TLS 1.2+)
